articles.authors: Daniel Mark Low; Osiris Rankin; Daniel D.L. Coppersmith; Kate Bentley; Matthew Nock; Satrajit S Ghosh
articles.abstract: Researchers often want to measure a variety of constructs such as anxiety, discrimination, or loneliness in text data from surveys, interviews, social media, and electronic health records. Using large language models (LLMs) --while optimal for text classification-- remain infeasible for some researchers due to concerns around computational expertise, cost, privacy, and compute requirements. Therefore, some researchers prefer to use lightweight models for large datasets or interpretable models to avoid mistakes in high-stakes scenarios such as suicide risk detection. Lexicons offer simple baselines to LLMs by searching for relevant phrases --and can be used together with LLMs to guarantee capturing specific keywords in a deterministic way. However, building new lexicons is resource intensive. In this study, we found that GPT-4 turbo was able to automatically create a lexicon for 49 known risk factors for suicidal thoughts and behaviors, which we release as the Suicide Risk Lexicon. Generating a lexicon with LLMs quickly measures most constructs relevant for suicide risk detection, resulting in high content validity. This lexicon was able to accurately predict risk in crisis counseling conversations. After validating the lexicon with clinician ratings, the lexicon modestly outperformed the LIWC lexicon --which has low content validity for mental illness-- and performed similarly to some black-box deep learning models. Due to using an interpretable approach with high content validity, we discovered that active suicidal ideation and direct self-injury were stronger indicators of imminent risk than passive suicidal ideation and depressed mood in this ecological setting  (i.e., naturalistic counseling conversations as the crisis is happening, as opposed to assessments or interviews about retrospective symptoms). To simplify creating new lexicons for other research domains, we introduce a Python package, construct-tracker, that works with a variety of LLMs. In sum, while we recommend using LLMs for text classification, they remain out of reach for many researchers. Our work demonstrates that LLMs --despite being black-boxes-- can counterintuitively create interpretable models by generating lexicons, when this is preferred. However, more studies are needed to test how these methods generalize to other datasets and psychological domains. Furthermore, we highlight the broader application of lexicons beyond measurement, including their use in benchmarking LLM performance.

articles.title: Distinct neural representations of perceptual and numerical absence in the human brain
articles.authors: Benjy Barnett; Stephen M Fleming
articles.abstract: The ability to represent absences is fundamental to human perception and cognition. Sensitivity to perceptual absences depends on inferring a lack of sensory evidence, enabling the recognition of a lack of environmental resources or other objects. In turn, encoding absence as a concept – in the form of the number zero – has enabled a multitude of technological and mathematical advances. Whether perceptual and conceptual absences rely on overlapping or distinct neural representations in the human brain, however, remains unknown. We employed three different tasks together with magnetoencephalography (MEG) to characterise neural representations of absence evoked within perceptual and numerical domains. Using multivariate decoding and Bayes factor analyses, we replicate previous findings of shared representations of numerical absence across symbolic and non-symbolic formats. In contrast, we show that these representations of numerical absence are distinct to those evoked by perceptual absences. This result held even after controlling for stimulus-level confounds which generate surface-level similarities between perceptual absences and non-symbolic empty sets. Our results extend a boundary between perception and cognition to the domain of absence and provide insight into the different mechanisms employed by the human brain to both perceive and conceive of absences.

articles.title: Articulatory kinematics during atypical stop productions in Parkinson’s disease
articles.authors: Micah E. Hirsch; Daria Dragicevic; Daphne Toglia; Manuel Diaz Cadiz; Cara Stepp
articles.abstract:
Background: People with Parkinson’s disease (PD) often produce more atypical stop consonant productions (with either absent or multiple bursts) compared to their healthy control counterparts. However, the physiological underpinnings of these productions are not well understood. Therefore, the primary aim of this study was to examine the articulatory correlates of atypical stop consonant production in speakers with PD.
Methods: A total of 30 speakers with PD read a passage in their typical speaking voice while electromagnetic articulography sensors tracked lip and tongue movement. The number of bursts and classification of stop types (prototypical, absent, and multiple bursts) were determined by trained research assistants based on the speech acoustics. The number of articulatory releases and the magnitude of release were determined from the tracings of the lip and alveolar apertures. Generalized mixed-effects models were used to determine whether the number of articulatory releases significantly differed between prototypical and atypical stop productions. Linear mixed-effects models were used to determine whether the magnitude of release was significantly different between prototypical and atypical stop productions.
Results: There were no significant differences in the number or magnitudes of articulatory releases between prototypical stop productions and atypical productions (e.g., absent and multiple burst productions).
Conclusions: These results do not support that articulatory kinematics are the driving force behind these atypical stop productions in PD. Future research should investigate the influence of other speech subsystems on these productions.

articles.title: Global environmental ideologies cluster into four distinct cognitive archetypes
articles.authors: Yuwei Kong; Sacha Epskamp
articles.abstract: Standardized assessments of environmental attitudes typically assume a universal psychological structure, where populations differ in the degree of concern but not in the organization of beliefs. Here we challenge this assumption by applying network analysis to data from 35,617 individuals across 28 countries and regions. We show that global environmental ideologies do not follow a singular logic but cluster into four distinct topological archetypes. The Western Core cluster (including the USA, Australia, and most Western European nations) exhibits a polarized structure organized around environmental skepticism, where environmental protection is positioned against economic priorities. The East Asian cluster (Mainland China, Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan) displays a network with weak conditional associations despite high edge density, suggesting that environmental attitudes are shaped more by social norms than by internal cognitive consistency. The Transitional Economy cluster (Hungary, Russia, Thailand, and South Africa) is characterized by a moderately dense network centered on environmental skepticism, forming a coherent belief package. Finally, the Global South cluster (India and the Philippines) shows the sparsest network structure, dominated by a strong link between perceived helplessness and low environmental priority. These results demonstrate that low psychometric consistency in non-Western contexts often reflects meaningful structural adaptation rather than measurement error. We conclude that effective climate communication depends on leveraging these diverse cognitive architectures rather than imposing a one-size-fits-all Western model.

articles.title: Ways of Comforting: Examining Self-Recognition and Self-Identification in Self-Compassion During Dialogues with AI-Generated Child Replicas
articles.authors: Diego Brandenberger; Paulina Zybinska; Jonas Wiesendanger; Roxane Laetitia Frund; Birgit Kleim; Bigna Lenggenhager; Marte Roel Lesur
articles.abstract: Recent advances in virtual reality (VR) and generative artificial intelligence (AI) enable highly realistic encounters with one’s externalized self, creating new opportunities to study and potentially foster self-compassion. Prior work suggests that self-externalization can increase self-compassion, yet the relative contributions of self-recognition and self-identification remain unclear. We therefore examined how manipulations of self-recognition and self-identification influence explicit and implicit indices of self-compassion during an immersive VR-based comforting task. In two within-subject studies, participants provided verbal comfort to an emotionally responsive virtual child in an immersive environment. In Study 1 (N = 24), self-recognition was manipulated using AI-generated deepfake videos depicting either the participant’s childhood face (high self-recognition) or an unfamiliar child (low self-recognition). In Study 2 (N = 20), self-identification was targeted by manipulating visuo-tactile synchrony: participants received shoulder touches that were either synchronous or asynchronous with the observed touch to the virtual child. Explicit measures included self-compassion and self-criticism questionnaires.  Implicit measures included heart rate variability (HRV) and acoustic vocal features as physiological proxies of compassionate responding. Across both studies, self-compassion increased from pre- to post-intervention, accompanied by reduced self-criticism according to questionnaire measures. In Study 1, the intervention was also associated with increased HRV (RMSSD) which has been linked to increased self-compassion. However, neither manipulation produced robust differences between experimental and control conditions on primary outcomes. On the other hand, self-recognition was linked to a greater degree of physical and emotional closeness towards the child, connectedness to oneself, negative affect, and less defined body boundaries. Together, the findings suggest that a brief immersive VR-based self-externalization task can reliably enhance self-compassion, whereas self-recognition may modulate other affective and bodily processes.

articles.title: A Reversal of Visual and Semantic Dominance in Scene Representations from Perception and Short-Term Memory to Long-Term Memory
articles.authors: Adva Shoham; Itay Yaron; Liad Mudrik; Galit Yovel
articles.abstract: Scene images encapsulate complex information, integrating both perceptual features and semantic meaning. It is therefore difficult to disentangle their unique contributions in scene mental representations. Moreover, the relative contributions of visual and semantic information may vary across different cognitive systems. To quantify the unique contributions of visual and semantic information to scene representations, we leveraged visual and language deep learning models, to capture pure visual information from scene images and pure semantic information of the textual descriptions of their categories, respectively. Using representational similarity analysis, we measured the unique variance these visual and semantic models explain in the representational geometry of visual scene representations across perception, short-term memory (STM) and long-term memory (LTM).  We found that scene representations were visually dominated in perception, while they were semantically dominated in LTM. The relative contributions of visual and semantic information to the representations in STM were similar to perception but differed from LTM. Using the same approach, we replicated the visual dominance in perception and STM in participants’ drawings of scene images. Taken together, these findings reveal a reversal of visual and semantic dominance between perception and LTM and demonstrate that visual working memory maintains primarily visual codes. This framework offers a powerful new way to separate and quantify visual and semantic contributions, which are typically intermixed in human mental representations, advancing our understanding on how visual knowledge is represented across different cognitive systems in the mind and brain.

articles.title: Examining predictors of adaptation to a non-uniform formant perturbation and the construct validity of assays of sensory acuity
articles.authors: Hung-Shao Cheng; Timothy Kevin Murphy; Benjamin Parrell; Caroline A Niziolek
articles.abstract:
Purpose: Interindividual differences in the magnitude of adaptation to non-uniform formant perturbations have been consistently observed, yet individual characteristics that can account for these differences remain poorly understood. Here, we assess whether the sensory acuity and production variability measures that were proposed to predict adaptation to simple, uniform perturbations also predict adaptation to complex, non-uniform ones. We additionally assess the construct validity of these auditory and somatosensory acuity measures. 
Method: 78 participants completed a battery of assessments of auditory acuity, somatosensory acuity of the tongue, and vowel production variability. Subsequently, participants produced words containing the four American English corner vowels while exposed to a non-uniform centralization perturbation pushing vowel formants towards the center of the vowel space. Adaptation was measured globally as changes in vowel spacing (the average distance between each vowel pair) and for each vowel individually.
Results: Average vowel formant variability was the only significant predictor of overall adaptation, with less variable participants adapting more. This finding partially aligned with vowel-specific results, where variability predicted adaptive changes for /u/ but not other vowels. None of the sensory acuity measures predicted adaptation either overall or for any individual vowel. Auditory acuity was moderately correlated across the four corner vowels and for pure tones; somatosensory acuity was not correlated across tasks. Higher auditory acuity predicted lower production variability overall, but not for any individual vowel.
Conclusion: Our results, though mixed, suggest that individual characteristics in production ability better predict adaptation to a non-uniform formant perturbation task than sensory acuity, contrary to previous findings for uniform perturbations which have mainly found a relationship between adaptation and auditory acuity. This suggests that task differences may influence the degree to which individual-level factors predict sensorimotor adaptation in speech.

articles.title: Mesmer’s error: Beliefs reverse the rubber hand illusion (a Registered Report)
articles.authors: Peter Lush; Zoltan Dienes
articles.abstract: In the rubber hand illusion (RHI), participants report ownership of a fake hand which is stroked in synchrony with their own concealed hand, with lower ownership ratings for asynchronous stroking. This effect has been historically attributed to multisensory integration. However, such a pattern of results is consistent with experimental demand characteristics, and an alternative theory proposes that the RHI is an effect of phenomenological control (PC; the trait ability to change experience to satisfy goals). To date, support for a PC theory of the RHI has been limited to correlational evidence. Here we conduct a pre-registered causal test of the relative contribution of these two proposed mechanisms in RHI effects. 25 high PC participants (top 15%) were tested in an RHI procedure and the classic effects of greater response for synchrony than asynchrony were replicated. The procedure was then repeated following imaginative suggestions for a reversal of the classic effect (i.e. greater synchrony than asynchrony effects). Ratings of ownership and proprioceptive drift were modulated by suggestion. Effects for ownership and drift were reversed. Exploratory analysis showed the same pattern of results for referred touch ratings. Participant beliefs arising from demand characteristics dominate over multisensory mechanisms in RHI ownership of a fake hand and proprioceptive drift. Multisensory synchrony is neither necessary nor sufficient for the rubber hand illusion. Claims of an illusion beyond that attributable to demand characteristics and phenomenological control require experimental evidence from controlled experiments

articles.title: How human-like is LLM cognition?
articles.authors: Zak Hussain; Rui Mata; Dirk U. Wulff
articles.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have led to renewed debate about whether artificial systems might possess human-like cognition. Although empirical evidence is growing quickly, it remains difficult to synthesize because studies vary in terms of which kinds of evidence they compare across humans and LLMs—design specifications, internal states, or behavior—and which kinds of inferences they draw. We address this challenge by organizing the Human-LLM comparison literature into broad evaluation categories and prototypical patterns of findings that either support or challenge LLM human-likeness. The resulting picture is one of jagged alignment, with human-likeness varying across dimensions rather than holding globally. We argue that studying these similarities and differences can clarify not only the functioning of artificial systems, but also the conceptual foundations of cognitive science.

articles.title: Assembly Instructions for the Modular Mind
articles.authors: Kartik Chandra; Jonathan Ragan-Kelley; Joshua Tenenbaum; Rebecca Saxe
articles.abstract: How do domain-specific cognitive systems interface with knowledge from beyond their respective domains? In this paper, we draw on the notion of domain-specificity in programming language theory to provide a computational account of how domain-specific systems might interface with domain-general world knowledge, as well as with other domain-specific systems.

articles.title: A Computational Theory of Dignity
articles.authors: Kartik Chandra; Joshua Tenenbaum; Rebecca Saxe
articles.abstract: People seem to hold a variety of conflicting intuitions about the concept of dignity. Here, we seek to reverse-engineer the computational basis of these intuitions. We propose a Bayesian model that casts intuitions about dignity as computations about what agents' actions imply about their own and others' social rank. We show through two behavioral experiments that our model captures people's intuitions well, reconciling seemingly conflicting intuitions with a common computational framework.

articles.title: How does work-based training assist individuals with learning disabilities in gaining meaningful employment in the UK: A Systematic Review.
articles.authors: Toni Joanne Southern; Stephen Dunne; Toby Brandon; Dale Metcalfe
articles.abstract:
Background: Employment rates for people with a learning disability remain low. Working collaboratively with people with lived experience and utilising co-productive practices can promote inclusivity, whilst work-based training can improve employability. This review examined work-based interventions for individuals with learning disabilities in the UK, any co-productive practices utilised in research design, and the interventions' impact on employability.

Methods: This review was pre-registered with the systematic review repository PROSPERO. A systematic search across ProQuest, Web of Science, and Scopus for studies published between 1st April 2012 and 1st January 2026 yielded seven eligible studies that explored training strategies, participant experiences, and factors influencing employability. Data were synthesised narratively. Study quality was assessed using the MMAT tool.

Findings: Key themes included person-centred approaches, high expectations, and appropriate support. Increased social connectedness and self-esteem were indicators of successful interventions. Employment outcomes were associated with work placement duration, age and disability characteristics. No studies incorporated co-production. 

Conclusion: Work-based interventions can improve employability for people with learning disabilities. Strategies that align with collaborative working are implemented to increase successful outcomes, yet co-productive practices are not found in any of the included literature. These findings underscore the need to embed co-productive practices in future research.

articles.title: “Do as I Say, Not as I Do” - The role of hypocrisy metaperceptions in conversations about pro-environmental change
articles.authors: Tabea Hoffmann; Lukas Robert Braun; Anja Heske; Neža Hrovat; Elena Manchorova; Georgia Petridou; Nurlana Ismayilzada; Maria Ilie-Niculescu
articles.abstract: Pro-environmental change necessitates discussions about the environment, yet many people avoid such conversations. We propose that this reluctance may partly stem from hypocrisy metaperceptions: because environmental issues are inherently moral and discrepancies between concern and action are common, advocating for behavioral change without acting accordingly may be associated with concerns about being perceived as hypocritical. We investigated whether inducing feelings of hypocrisy reduces willingness to talk about environmental solutions, and whether this differs for individual-level versus system-level change. In a multinational online experiment (N = 957) across six European countries, participants were randomly assigned to a hypocrisy induction or control condition and reported their willingness to discuss individual- and system-level environmental solutions, as well as their metaperceptions of appearing hypocritical. While the hypocrisy induction did not significantly affect metaperceptions or willingness to talk, structural equation modeling revealed a negative association between hypocrisy metaperceptions and willingness to discuss both individual- and system-level change. Our findings suggest that the anticipated social costs of appearing hypocritical may contribute to the silence surrounding environmental discourse, potentially undermining the very conversations needed to drive both individual behavior change and system-level change on environmental issues.

articles.title: Can personality be deliberately changed? Effects of a randomized, placebo-controlled intervention on character strengths and Big Five personality traits
articles.authors: Lisa Wagner; Valentina Vylobkova; Sonja Heintz; Jessie Sun; Fabian Gander
articles.abstract: The promise of intentional change in adult personality traits attracts broad interest, even as convincing evidence remains scarce and methodologically challenging. In a preregistered randomized placebo- and waitlist-controlled study with N = 3,434 adults, we tested whether an eight-week online training of a self-selected character strength produces short- and long-term changes in character strengths (states and traits) and Big Five personality traits. Participants in the intervention condition increased targeted strengths states more than both placebo and waitlist control conditions. However, intervention and placebo participants experienced similar gains in targeted strengths traits, extraversion, and agreeableness, as well as similar decreases in neuroticism relative to the waitlist condition. Thus, some effects may result from non-specific aspects of the structured activity. Informants did not observe any between-condition differences in strengths trait change. These findings underscore the importance of using rigorous control conditions in research on volitional personality change.

articles.title: Imagery Interventions and Cognitive-Behavioural Therapy for Psychological Complaints related to Bullying: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
articles.authors: Meng Liang
articles.abstract:
Background: Bullying is associated with internalising symptoms and impaired everyday functioning. Cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT) and imagery interventions have been studied as treatments to improve mental health after such experiences.
Objectives: This meta-analysis aimed to evaluate the effects of individual CBT and imagery interventions in targeting internalising symptoms (negative affect, depression, anxiety and post traumatic symptom ) and improving self-perception and coping in bullying victims.
Methods: Systematic searches of PubMed, PsycInfo, Embase, and Web of Science, identified 23 studies applying CBT (n = 15) and imagery interventions (n = 8) in bullying victims. Risk of bias and random-effects meta-analyses were conducted for pre-post and pre-follow-up comparisons. Sensitivity analyses and meta-regressions explored potential moderators.
Results: Significant reductions in internalising symptoms were observed from pre- to post-intervention (g = 0.72), with imagery interventions (g = 0.89) and CBT interventions (g = 0.62) demonstrating medium to high effect sizes. Follow-up assessments (k = 9) suggested that symptom reduction was maintained (g = 0.53). Significant improvements in self-perception and coping outcomes were also observed (g = 0.82). Sensitivity analyses identified specific studies contributing to large heterogeneity (I2: 94–98%). Number-Needed-to-Treat (NNT) values increased sharply after bias correction, ranging from 3.11 to 12.67, indicating more modest clinical benefits than initially estimated.
Conclusions: When considering the available literature, the findings suggest that both CBT and imagery interventions demonstrate potential efficacy in reducing internalising symptoms and are associated with more positive self-perception and coping in bullying victims. These findings provide preliminary support for the potential benefits of structured psychological interventions to improve mental health following bullying experiences. However, large heterogeneity and publication bias warrant cautious interpretation and indicate a need for high-quality, manualized controlled trials to confirm clinical efficacy.

articles.title: Semantic properties and categorization norms for the 260 Snodgrass and Vanderwart objects: a 45-year conceptual update to a classic set
articles.authors: Caitlyn Antal; Roberto G. de Almeida
articles.abstract: We present a new set of English property norms for 260 object concepts based on the standardized Snodgrass and Vanderwart (1980) picture set. For each object, 100 participants provided a basic-level label (dog), a superordinate category (animal), and three features (bark, tail, fur), yielding a dataset of 78,000 features. Our norms differ from other datasets in four important ways: they (1) probe basic-level information, (2) separate taxonomic and feature information, (3) use open-ended responses for natural descriptions; and (4) include a larger number of responses per object (100 vs. ~30 participants in other norms). We analyzed feature statistics such as frequency, distinctiveness, and co-occurrence, and contrasted our norms with those of McRae et al. (2005), CSLB (Devereux et al., 2014), and Hovhannisyan et al. (2021). Compared to picture-based norms, our data—derived from black-and-white line drawings—elicited more diverse features and aligned more closely with language-based norms, particularly CSLB. We assessed the generalizability of our norms using an object-property congruency task, where 144 participants judged whether properties (basic level, superordinate, features) were related to objects. Objects were shown in three picture formats with increasing ecological validity: (1) colored line drawings, (2) realistic photographs, and (3) realistic photographs of objects in scenes. Agreement rates for object-property pairs remained high across picture formats and property types. Bayesian inference revealed minimal variability in congruency judgments across picture formats, with responses tightly clustered around zero. Results show that our norms are generalizable to realistic visual stimuli. Norms are available at https://osf.io/c6brw/overview.

articles.title: A luminance-induced expansion aftereffect
articles.authors: Harsh Vashistha; Damon Clark
articles.abstract: We report a novel luminance-induced expansion aftereffect in which adaptation to sawtooth luminance modulation with a slow decreasing phase causes subsequently viewed static patterns to appear to expand. Eight observers were adapted to luminance ramps and judged the appearance of subsequent static and contracting test patterns. The magnitude of this expansion aftereffect was quantified using a nulling paradigm; the illusory expansion was cancelled when the test pattern contracted at ~ 0.3°/s. This expansion aftereffect is qualitatively distinct from the brightening aftereffect previously associated with sawtooth luminance adaptation, suggesting dissociable perceptual consequences of luminance-change adaptation for brightness and spatial coding.

articles.title: VPP: An Open-Source Blockchain-based System for Verifiable Research-Participation Credit Management
articles.authors: Joris Strakeljahn; Jasmin Rabe; Alexander Haubrock; Gerrit Hirschfeld
articles.abstract: Managing research-participation credits is a common administrative task in psychology departments. Existing solutions rely on centralized, identity-linked databases that store students’ personal data and require operator trust for record integrity. We present Verifiable research Participation credit (VPP), an open-source system that records participation credits as tamper-resistant, pseudonymous transactions on the blockchain. VPP requires no prior exposure to blockchain technology by individual staff or students. Wallets are created upon first use in the browser and all transaction fees are covered by the institution. Credits are tied to cryptographic wallet addresses rather than to identities, which implements data minimization by design. We describe the architecture and provide a structured feature comparison with existing tools along data sovereignty, verifiability, privacy, cost, and openness. Technical validation comprised a smart-contract test suite, a transaction-cost analysis (approximately 2 euros per thousand claims plus a low one-off setup), and a structured threat-model analysis. An initial usability assessment with N = 45 students using the System Usability Scale yielded a mean of M = 65.89 (SD = 15.84), close to the canonical SUS baseline of 68. Both the technical and the usability evaluation show that the wallet-based interaction model is viable for psychology students at first encounter and during subsequent use. All source code, documentation, and deployment guides are available under an MIT license. VPP is intended as a complement or alternative to existing platforms for departments that prioritize data minimization and independent record verifiability.

articles.title: Mother-infant behavioral synchrony and maternal cortisol accumulation predict later infant hair cortisol levels
articles.authors: Isis Angelica Segura; Isabela Macen Petrovitch de Oliveira; Julia Carnauba Terra; Murilo Correa; Beatriz Pacheco; Camila Fragoso Ribeiro; Patrícia Pereira Araújo; Miriam Ribeiro; Ana Alexandra Caldas Osório
articles.abstract: Early-life exposure to sustained stress is associated with several adverse developmental outcomes. Research on acute cortisol measures has identified maternal cortisol regulation and behavioral synchrony - defined as the coordination of mother–infant behaviors during interactions - as important contributors to infant stress regulation. However, their interplay with chronic stress, as indexed by hair cortisol concentration (HCC), remains unexplored. This study investigated how maternal HCC and mother-infant behavioral synchrony relate to infants’ HCC accumulation from 9 to 12 months. Behavioral synchrony was assessed at 6 months of age (N=76 dyads). Video-recorded 3-minute interactions were coded using the Coding Interactive Behavior system, yielding composites of maternal sensitivity, intrusiveness, and dyadic reciprocity. At 12 months, 3-cm hair samples were collected from mothers and infants to assess cortisol accumulation over the previous three months. A General Linear Model showed that lower maternal HCC and higher maternal sensitivity predicted lower infant HCC, while the effect of maternal intrusiveness on infant HCC was fully accounted for by socioeconomic status. The effect of dyadic reciprocity was marginally significant. These findings highlight maternal chronic cortisol and behavioral synchrony as independent predictors of infants’ chronic cortisol accumulation, providing novel evidence linking relational processes and chronic stress biomarkers in infancy.

articles.title: LLMs Struggle With Negation, but so do Humans - a Two-step Simulation Approach
articles.authors: Lenny Döcker; Kevin Erens; Barbara Kaup; Francesca Capuano
articles.abstract: While substantial research has addressed the challenges of negation processing in humans, the parallels between human cognitive models and large language models (LLMs) in this regard remain less explored. This paper investigates how GPT-4o processes negation, drawing comparisons to human negation processing, particularly the two-step model of negation. In two experiments using image-sentence pairs, we assess the model’s ability to handle affirmative and negated sentences. Our findings reveal that, like humans, GPT-4o struggles more with negation, exhibiting higher rates of incorrect inferences after negation, and a systematic bias toward completions associated with the negated state of affairs. This study highlights the qualitative similarities between AI and human processing of negation, offering insights into the limitations of current LLMs and suggesting future directions for improving their cognitive alignment with more complex linguistic constructs.

articles.title: Do Large Language Models Show Negation Bias? A Replication of Beukeboom et al. (2010)
articles.authors: Leonie-Marie Allar; Ann-Sophie Berg; Barbara Kaup; Francesca Capuano
articles.abstract:
LLM bias research has largely focused on content-level associations, overlooking biases arising from linguistic form and pragmatic choice. One such phenomenon is the negation bias, where negated expressions are preferentially used for stereotype-inconsistent behavior and trigger systematic inferences. While LLMs’ handling of negation in semantic tasks is well studied, it is unclear whether they reproduce these pragmatic inferences in social contexts. We replicate Beukeboom et al. (2010) with Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2, varying the valence and polarity of trait descriptions. The model inferred more negative expectations from negated negative traits (e.g. not stupid) and more positive expectations from negated positive traits (e.g. not smart), and showed stronger situational but weaker dispositional attributions. These results mirror human negation-bias patterns, suggesting that instruction-tuned models can produce pragmatic-like bias in judgment tasks. The findings underscore
the need to extend LLM bias evaluation beyond content to linguistic form.

articles.title: Anticipated Stigma is Associated with Lower Healthcare Access and Longer Diagnostic Delay in LGBTQ+ Patients with Endometriosis Symptoms
articles.authors: Ellen Frost; Madeleine Pownall; Daryl Brian O'Connor
articles.abstract:
Objective: It is important to understand the barriers that lead to an endometriosis diagnosis, to reduce the diagnostic delay. This study aimed to examine whether endometriosis-related anticipated stigma is associated with healthcare access and diagnostic delay in an LGBTQ+ sample. Sexual orientation concealment and conformity to feminine norms were explored as moderators.
Methods: This study was a quantitative design. 145 participants who identified as a sexual orientation minority (including gender minorities) with endometriosis symptoms, completed an online questionnaire. Linear regression, moderation, and moderated moderation analyses examined the relationships between variables.
Results: Participants waited on average eight years for a diagnosis after first visiting a healthcare provider. Higher levels of endometriosis-related anticipated stigma were associated with lower levels of healthcare access and longer clinical diagnostic delay. There was a moderating effect of sexual orientation concealment on the relationship between endometriosis-related anticipated stigma and clinical diagnosis time dependent on levels of conformity to feminine norms related to romantic relationships.
Conclusions: Anticipated endometriosis stigma delays accessing care and diagnosis. This relationship may be shaped by societal structures that systematically marginalise certain populations. Interventions to reduce delays should consider demographic factors such as sexual orientation to ensure interventions are effective for all with endometriosis.

articles.title: Diffusion-Weighted Imaging of Gray Matter Microstructural Profiles in Healthy Aging
articles.authors: Jenna Louise Merenstein
articles.abstract: Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) is a non-invasive magnetic resonance imaging technique that can provide detailed information about brain microstructure. This methodology has historically been applied only to white matter tissue, but more recent literature suggests that DWI can also be applied to study microstructural alterations of gray matter tissue, including in healthy aging. The current narrative review evaluates DWI studies investigating age-related differences in gray matter microstructure and their implications for understanding cognitive function in healthy older adults. The studies reviewed here report widespread age-related differences in cortical (especially frontoparietal), hippocampal, and subcortical microstructure that is behaviorally relevant– especially for cognitive domains of learning and memory. Gray matter microstructure is consistently influenced by the presence of dementia-related pathology, physical activity, and sleep health, but additional research work is needed to understand the roles of biological sex, environment, and dietary intake. Future work of gray matter microstructural profiles in healthy aging should consider longitudinal designs, combining different DWI modeling techniques in the same study, and defining more specific cognitive correlates (e.g., processing speed).

articles.title: Alternative-Space Structure Shapes Negation Resolution in Multimodal Generation
articles.authors: Francesca Capuano; Elisa Berger; Sophia Weber; Barbara Kaup
articles.abstract: Negation in natural language is not interpreted as a simple logical complement but is resolved selectively, depending on how linguistic and conceptual structure constrain relevant alternatives. Psycholinguistic research suggests that negation facilitates consideration of an alternative only when a privileged counterstate is available, either through contextual restriction or graded prominence. We use text-to-image generation as a complementary method to probe this selectivity under forced representational commitment: when negated language must be rendered visually, a concrete state must be instantiated. Across two preregistered experiments, participants evaluated how well generated images matched affirmative and negated prompts. Experiment 1 shows that negation is resolved more successfully for predicates encoding conventionalized contrasts than for those with open alternative spaces. Experiment 2 shows that, within open spaces, negation is facilitated when a highly prominent alternative exists. Together, the results support an account in which binary advantages arise from how strongly a counterstate is privileged.

articles.title: Why Intentions Fail to Become Action: A Threshold Account of Action Initiation
articles.authors: Nikesh Lagun; Binita Lungba Tamang; Kushal Budha; Sunam Darji; Bishow Ghimire; Sakshi Adhikari; Samrakshan Rijal
articles.abstract: People often intend to act, recognize why the action matters, and still do not begin. This article argues that such failures reveal an under-specified transition in psychological theories of behavior: the moment at which intended action becomes capable of onset. Existing theories explain central parts of the action sequence, including intention formation, implementation planning, self-regulation, effort allocation, and habit. Yet behavioral onset is often treated as a downstream consequence of these processes rather than as a transition with its own enabling conditions. This article develops a threshold account of action initiation. On this account, intention can orient the person toward action, but onset occurs only when effective activation exceeds a resistance- and state-sensitive boundary. Initiation, therefore, depends not only on wanting or planning an action but also on readiness, execution compatibility, attentional stabilization, initiation resistance, structured instability, and prior trajectory. Integrating evidence from intention-behavior research, implementation intentions, procrastination, educational delay, health behavior, digital engagement, and within-person variability, the article distinguishes intention from action possibility and onset from persistence. The account generates falsifiable predictions concerning conditional intention effects, nonlinear onset, resistance manipulation, execution compatibility, path dependence, and structured variability. Its contribution is to specify when valued and intended actions become capable of beginning.

articles.title: PTSD risk detection from trauma narratives using natural language processing
articles.authors: Giuliano Groer; Laurin Plank; Armin Zlomuzica
articles.abstract:
Machine learning techniques are gaining recognition for their significant potential in the
early identification and diagnosis of trauma-related disorders. With this study we
investigated if natural language processing (NLP) could be utilized to analyse
individuals’ trauma narratives to identify PTSD-risk. We developed a classifier to
predict individual risk of developing PTSD based on PCL-5 questionnaire scores, using
semantic embeddings derived from autobiographical narratives describing participants’
most traumatic life events. Participants (N = 150) provided narratives about their most
traumatic, most negative, and neutral events, along with self-report data on
trauma-related symptomatology (PTSD Checklist for DSM-5, PCL-5), anxiety and
depression levels (Depression, Anxiety, and Stress Scale, DASS-21). Semantic
embedding models, based on modern NLP approaches, were used to numerically
represent the semantic content of narratives. The resulting semantic representations
were used to train a statistical classification model, and model performance was
evaluated using cross-validation procedures. The classifier trained on trauma narratives
achieved significantly improved PTSD-risk prediction performance when compared to a
majority class classifier (AUC = 0.70, p = 0.002). No significant prediction was
achieved when classifiers were trained on negative or neutral narratives instead of
traumatic narratives, highlighting the unique role of traumatic memories in enabling the
detection of elevated PTSD risk. Importantly, the classifier based on trauma narratives
selectively predicted trauma-related symptoms (PCL-5) but not depression and anxiety
symptomatology (assessed by the DASS-21 subscales), indicating its unique ability to
leverage trauma- and PTSD-specific features for classification. Finally, interpretability
analyses revealed increased anxiety expressions and first-person pronoun usage might
drive the classifier’s detection of elevated PTSD risk. Our findings support the
usefulness of machine learning approaches that utilize linguistic feature markers from
autobiographical trauma memory narratives for the selective and robust early detection
of trauma-related psychopathology.

articles.title: Cognitive Abilities, Personality, and Sustainable Attitudes and Behavior
articles.authors: Till Lubczyk; Wiebke Bleidorn; Christopher James Hopwood; André Kretzschmar
articles.abstract:
Climate change demands urgent action, yet individual engagement in sustainable
behaviors remains inconsistent. To better understand the factors that promote sustainable
behaviors, we conducted two studies (N₁ = 280, N₂ = 973) examining how political
orientation, personality traits, reasoning ability, social-emotional-behavioral skills, and
complex problem solving relate to sustainable attitudes and behaviors in U.S. adults. Using
structural equation modeling, we found that openness to experience was the strongest
predictor, alongside liberal political orientation and self-perceived social engagement and
innovation skills. Unexpectedly, both reasoning ability and complex problem solving showed
consistent negative associations with sustainability outcomes, challenging assumptions that
cognitive abilities universally facilitate adaptive behavior. Psychological characteristics
together explained approximately one-third of variance in both sustainable attitudes and
behaviors. Personality traits dominated explanation of attitudes, while behaviors emerged
from more balanced contributions across psychological domains, with social-emotional skills
providing meaningful incremental validity beyond established predictors. These findings
demonstrate that personality psychology provides crucial insights for understanding
environmental engagement, revealing that adaptive social-emotional competencies and
personality characteristics are more strongly associated with sustainability than cognitive
abilities.

articles.title: “It’s Ok—Not Everyone Has to Be an Einstein”: How Unequal Treatment in the Classroom Gets into Children’s Heads
articles.authors: Eddie Brummelman; Nathalie Aerts; Laura Naemi Alders; Hannah Armstrong; Eva Blokland; Mirna Đurić; Stefanie Nelemans; Lena-Emilia Schenker; Jellie Sierksma; Niels Vullings
articles.abstract: Achievement inequality is a pressing societal problem: Children from low socioeconomic status (SES) backgrounds underperform in school, even when they have the same ability as their high-SES peers. Why? In this article, we offer one piece of the puzzle by showing that children face unequal treatment in the classroom. The unequal treatment may be driven, in part, by a seemingly benign stereotype that portrays children from low-SES backgrounds as “strivers” or “go-getters” rather than naturally gifted. As a result of this stereotype, teachers may offer children from low-SES backgrounds inflated praise when they succeed, unsolicited help when they struggle, and pity when they fail. Such practices, although well-intentioned, may convey to children that they lack ability and are unable to cultivate their ability, which undermines their motivation and achievement. To counter these dynamics, interventions should target the pervasive societal beliefs—such as the belief in meritocracy—that sustain them.

articles.title: Ancient Influencers: The role of prestige bias in the appreciation of annotated historical text documents
articles.authors: Mirza Muchammad Iqbal; Alejandro Bahena-Rivera; Matthew L. Keegan; Kelsie G. Rodenbiker; Christoph Scheepers; Garrick V. Allen
articles.abstract:
Historical manuscripts are appreciated for their intellectual content and cultural importance, but their aesthetic and cognitive impacts, particularly for copies that contain post-production annotations, have received relatively little attention. Drawing on prestige bias theory, we tested whether author/annotator reputation and annotation relevance shape contemporary aesthetic engagement. The study employed a 2 × 2 × 2 factorial designs to orthogonally manipulate Author reputation (famous vs. non-famous), Annotator reputation (famous vs. non-famous) and type of Annotation (relevant vs. non-relevant). We used eight historical text documents, each with eight different preambles rotated through the design scheme via Latin Square. On each trial, participants (N = 430) first read the preamble (one of eight different conditions), then viewed the manuscript page at their own pace, and finally provided 7-point Likert ratings on 13 different aesthetic-affective evaluation dimensions. Results showed that participants looked longer at manuscripts labelled as being authored and annotated by famous people, compared to conditions where either the author or the annotator were less famous. Condensing the ratings into five orthogonal factors (Aesthetic Appeal, Gravitas, Positive Connection, Orderliness, and Negativity) revealed that relevant annotations were associated with higher Gravitas scores than non-relevant annotations. Additionally, the factor Positive Connection scored higher for famous authors, famous annotators, and relevant annotations. Our findings demonstrate that reputation (for both author and annotator) and annotation relevance shape contemporary responses to pre-modern manuscripts. These findings advance prestige bias theory in aesthetic domains and offer practical implications for museum curation and manuscript scholarship.

Keywords: Historical text; Annotation; Reading culture; Cognitive Psychology; Prestige

articles.title: The double dissociation between cortical blindness and aphantasia: a review of 55 cases
articles.authors: Stefano Zago; Dounia Hajhajate; Alice Naomi Preti; Teresa Difonzo; Silvia Cattaneo; Paolo Bartolomeo
articles.abstract: A long-standing assumption in cognitive neuroscience is that visual mental imagery (VMI) is “visual perception in reverse” and, as such, requires reactivation of the primary visual cortex (V1). On this view, total destruction of V1 should abolish VMI and provoke neurological aphantasia. We tested this prediction by reviewing 55 published cases of cortical blindness (CB) in which VMI abilities were assessed. Vivid VMI is repeatedly documented in CB patients with complete or near-complete bilateral destruction, disconnection, or functional loss of V1; in impaired-VMI cases with informative anatomical or functional evidence, the lesion extends well beyond V1, typically into the left temporal cortex. No impaired-VMI case with informative anatomical or functional evidence shows damage confined to V1. These data are incompatible with a necessary role for V1 in VMI and instead align with recent neuroimaging and lesion evidence pointing to a left temporal hub (the fusiform imagery node, FIN) together with domain-preferring ventral temporal regions, embedded within frontoparietal working memory and semantic networks. We outline practical guidelines for future neuropsychological studies of visual mental imagery in CB.

articles.title: Value-based decision-making and behavioural economic constructs that distinguish people with and without cannabis use history
articles.authors: Amelia J. Taylor; Brandon P. Miller; Amber Copeland
articles.abstract: Behavioural economic accounts posit distorted valuation processes underlie cannabis decisions; however, less is known about the moment-to-moment dynamics through which value influences observable choice behaviour. This study explored whether value-based decision-making and behavioural economic constructs distinguish people with and without cannabis use history. Sixty participants were recruited, half with history of cannabis use (cannabis group), and half without (control group). Participants completed a value-based decision-making task (choosing between previously rated cannabis-related and cannabis-unrelated images). A drift-diffusion model was fitted to behavioural data (choice and response time patterns) to estimate reinforcer-specific parameters, including evidence accumulation rates and response thresholds. Compared to controls, participants with cannabis use history had higher cannabis evidence accumulation rates (p = .023). Exploratory analyses revealed higher self-report cannabis demand across most indices (all except Pmax; ps ≤ .042). However, groups did not differ in cannabis-unrelated evidence accumulation rates or response thresholds for either decision type (ps ≥ .186). Notably, controls showed higher cannabis-unrelated than cannabis evidence accumulation rates (p = .001). People with a history of cannabis use ascribe a higher value to cannabis than those without such history, who in turn value cannabis less than alternative reinforcers—a pattern consistent with behavioural economic accounts of addiction.

articles.title: Recommendations for incorporating LLMs into psychological research: A Commentary on Austin and colleagues (2026)
articles.authors: Wendy C Higgins; Beth Clarke; Malte Elson; Jamie Cummins
articles.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have a lot of potential to be incorporated into psychological research methods. However, as with any new research method, LLM-based methods require rigorous evaluation. In this commentary, we critique the approach used by Austin and colleagues’ (2026) to evaluate the ability of two LLMs (ChatGPT 4o and Gemini 2.0 Flash) to facilitate scale validation. Specifically, Austin and colleagues used LLMs for two purposes: a) identifying relevant criterion measures, and b) generating validity hypotheses for assessing convergent and discriminant validity. We argue that their study has several key limitations and that, ultimately, their findings do not support their conclusion that these models can effectively assist with both of these tasks. We situate our critique within three broader recommendations for incorporating LLMs into research: (1) avoid essentialisation and overgeneralisation about “AI”, (2) use application programming interfaces when evaluating LLMs in research contexts, and (3) subject LLM-based methods to stringent evaluation.

articles.title: Using large language models to track national identities
articles.authors: Stefan Leach; Rebecca Gregson; Jan Nikadon; Magdalena Formanowicz; Chiara Zazzarino; Andrew Kitchin; Michal Kosinski; Jay Joseph Van Bavel; Aleksandra Cichocka
articles.abstract: National identity drives a range of political action, from civic engagement to intergroup violence. We present a novel and accessible approach using large language models (LLMs) to label texts for expressions of positive (national identification, patriotism) and defensive (nationalism, national narcissism) national identities. We test four popular LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Llama 4 Maverick) across 13 million words sourced from social media, surveys, and political speeches in 25 languages. Our findings reveal that LLMs label texts reliably and in a theoretically-consistent manner, producing judgments that closely match those of domain experts and outperform both dictionary-based approaches and crowdworkers—also reducing the cost by a factor of 1,000 compared to the latter. An analysis of US presidential addresses using the best-performing LLM (GPT-4o) reveals that expressions of national identities have doubled over the twentieth century. Further studies revealed differences between Republicans and Democrats. Defensive national identities were five times more prevalent in Republicans' social media posts than in Democrats'. Such identities were also frequent in the speeches of populist leaders around the globe. Together, these findings demonstrate that LLMs offer a reliable, valid, accessible, and cost-effective approach to labeling texts for nuanced expressions of national identity, enabling new insights into its role in contemporary and historical social trends.

articles.title: AI Coach: A Prototype of Artificially Intelligent Coach for Psychological Safety and Mental Wellbeing in Healthcare
articles.authors: Fakhra Jabeen; Jan Treur; Peter Roelofsma
articles.abstract: Patient healthcare and safety necessitates effective teamwork and collaboration, rooted in the concept of shared mental understanding or shared mental models. Different personal and professional factors among team members may influence patient diagnosis and decision-making, which can have long-lasting impact(s) on patient mental health and wellbeing. Our work introduces an innovative concept of an Artificially Intelligent (AI) Coach, designed to enhance the shared understanding and communication among team members. The aim is to reduce error expectancy in different medical fields (e.g., neonatology), by understanding complex behaviors during a neonatal process. This virtual assistant caters the information on error related causes or factors by monitoring them, and a related support is provided to minimize these errors. The coach encompasses computational logic regarding user tasks, offering monitoring and communication through a graphical user interface. This coach can play role in improving psychological safety of all the stakeholders like patients, medical practitioner or a neonate. During evaluation, our pilot study indicated that participants who acted as expert users with no prior knowledge of complex systems, were able to successfully design the computational models. These models were used for user-coach interaction by the end users. Furthermore, users indicated that user-coach interaction could be helpful in monitoring different causal factors and its related effects, i.e., it can be effective in terms of achieving its goal.

articles.title: Work-Related Psychosocial Hazards in UK and Ireland Construction: A Rapid Scoping Review to Inform Risk identification and Assessment
articles.authors: Carla Toro; David Nickson; Sebastian Ison-Jacques; Chloe Mitchell
articles.abstract: Work-related psychosocial hazards are increasingly recognised as modifiable determinants of stress and mental health. In Great Britain, the Health and Safety Executive Management Standards (HSE-MS) identify 6 areas of work design associated with work-related stress: Demands, Control, Support, Relationships, Role and Change. Surveillance of psychosocial hazards and mental health in the construction industry presents a distinctive challenge because work is temporary and project-based and delivered through fragmented supply chains. It remains unclear whether existing generic psychosocial risk assessment frameworks capture the hazards that need to be monitored in UK and Republic of Ireland (ROI) construction. This review aims to identify work-related psychosocial hazards reported in peer-reviewed studies of construction workers in the UK and ROI, map them against the HSE-MS, and assess their implications for construction-specific psychosocial risk identification and assessment. Three databases were searched for peer-reviewed studies published between 2000 and 2025. Eligible studies included UK or ROI construction workers and reported work-related psychosocial risk factors linked to mental health or wellbeing. Data were extracted on study characteristics, participant demographics, psychosocial hazards and equity-related variables. Hazards were mapped to HSE-MS domains and assessed as captured, partially captured or not captured by HSE Stress Indicator Tool wording. Hazards that did not fit a single domain were synthesised thematically with hazards not captured by the tool to identify surveillance-relevant gaps. Twelve studies were included. 112 coded hazard entries were mapped across the six HSE domains. 29 aligned clearly with Stress Indicator Tool wording, including long hours, high workload, time pressure, limited decision authority, poor support, interpersonal conflict, role conflict and poor communication around change. However, 81 hazards were not captured and two were partially captured. A further 21 hazards could not be mapped to a single HSE domain. These unmapped and not-captured hazards clustered into three surveillance-relevant categories: project-based commercial, programme, planning and delivery pressures; fragmented workforce structure, supply-chain position, financial insecurity and unequal access to protection; and delivery-first culture, weak worker voice and constrained recovery or help-seeking. The HSE-MS provide a useful foundation for psychosocial risk assessment in UK and ROI construction, but they do not fully capture hazards generated by construction’s project-based commercial model, fragmented employment arrangements and delivery-first culture. Construction-specific surveillance and risk assessment should retain the HSE domains while adding indicators of upstream procurement, programme, planning, payment, employment and supply-chain conditions.

articles.title: A One-Stage Approach for Estimating a Meta-Analytic Continuous-Time Random-Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model
articles.authors: Julian F Lohmann; Oliver Lüdtke; Alexander Robitzsch
articles.abstract: This article describes, evaluates, and applies a meta-analytic random-intercept cross-lagged panel model (RI-CLPM). The model enables meta-analytic study of temporal relations between two variables while separating stable between-person differences from within-person fluctuations. The RI-CLPM has been proposed as an alternative to the cross-lagged panel model and has recently motivated many reanalyzes of longitudinal datasets. To support flexible, integrative reanalysis across multiple datasets, we introduce a Bayesian one-stage approach for fitting a meta-analytic RI-CLPM using correlation matrices from longitudinal primary studies. The method combines Bayesian meta-analytic structural equation modeling with continuous-time modeling. The continuous-time framework allows us to account for different time lags across studies, a common challenge in longitudinal meta-analysis. We propose four model variants that differ in their random-effects structures for capturing between-study heterogeneity. A simulation study compares their performance, and an empirical example from educational psychology examines reciprocal relations between academic self-concept and achievement, illustrating their application.

articles.title: PREPRINT: Assessing chronic pain processes longitudinally: Psychometric evaluation of the German “Assessing Chronic Pain Processes” (ProCEss) item set
articles.authors: Verena Eunike Hofmann; Julia Glombiewski; Wiebke Heider; Saskia Scholten
articles.abstract:
Background. Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) is gaining relevance in contemporaneous pain research. However, EMA questionnaires are rarely psychometrically evaluated, potentially compromising data quality. This study aimed to develop and evaluate a German item set assessing psychological chronic pain processes for use in EMA, focusing on variability and reliability. 
Methods. First, the item set was piloted with three people suffering from chronic pain to gather feedback on participant burden and the usability of the data. Second, an evaluation was conducted with a sample of n = 68 individuals with lived experience of chronic pain who completed an EMA protocol with six assessments per day over twelve days (4,236 datapoints). Third, revised parts of the item set were preliminarily evaluated in n = 10 people suffering from chronic pain over a 21-day EMA period with six assessments per day (1,253 datapoints). Variability was examined primarily using within-person standard deviations and response distributions. Reliability was assessed according to current recommendations for single-item measures (test-retest reliability), two-item scales (Spearman-Brown coefficient), and multi-item scales (McDonald’s Omega).
Results. Most items and process scales met the predefined criteria for variability and reliability. However, variance was largely attributable to between-person differences. Four out of thirteen scales demonstrated insufficient reliability.
Conclusions. This study presents the first psychometrically evaluated German EMA item set capturing a broad range of clinically relevant pain processes. While the findings are promising, further refinement of several scales and additional psychometric evaluation are warranted.

articles.title: Subjective emotion judgements adhere to principles of Bayesian inference and efficient representation
articles.authors: Jade Serfaty; Quentin JM Huys
articles.abstract: Emotion judgements are central to wellbeing and mental health, yet the fundamental principles determining how they arise remain poorly characterised. A key challenge is that repeated emotion ratings show substantial variability, typically treated as noise. Here we propose that emotion judgements, like perceptual judgements, arise from probabilistic inference under representational constraints. Using behavioural and computational approaches, we show that this variability is structured and meaningful. Emotion judgements are biased toward prior expectations when evidence is weak, consistent with Bayesian inference, and representational precision scales with the frequency of experienced intensities, consistent with efficient coding. Confidence decreases as uncertainty increases, indicating partial awareness of this uncertainty. A single model combining efficient encoding with Bayesian decoding captures these behavioural signatures. Notably, these signatures were largely preserved across variation in anxiety symptoms. These findings link emotion self-report to the principles of perceptual inference, providing a computational account of how uncertainty structures emotion judgements.

articles.title: Domain-general cognition contributes to semantically challenging speech comprehension
articles.authors: Lucy Jane MacGregor; Fritz Peters; Judy Zhuo Chen; Adam Attaheri; Jennifer M Rodd; Matthew H. Davis
articles.abstract: Claims that domain-general cognitive mechanisms contribute to language comprehension are long-standing but controversial. Critics argue that involvement reflects general task demands rather than a functional role in core components of comprehension such as meaning access, selection, maintenance, and update. A functional role may be observable under conditions that increase demand on these psycholinguistic processes. Here, we focus on the challenge of semantic ambiguity resolution. We tested whether domain-general cognitive ability, operationalised through tests of fluid intelligence, contributes to comprehension of semantically ambiguous speech over and above lexico-semantic knowledge (measured with vocabulary tests). In two web experiments, healthy adult volunteers (n=67, n=100) listened to sentences containing a mid-sentence ambiguous word, critical for interpretation, which were disambiguated by a sentence-final word. After each utterance, participants heard a single word from the sentence. They defined the sentence-specific meaning of the word by typing (open response Meaning Definitions Task, Experiment 1) or selecting from four alternatives (4AFC Meaning Verification Task, Experiment 2). Accuracy measures showed good reliability. A principal components analysis of the cognitive measures confirmed two latent constructs reflecting lexico-semantic knowledge and domain-general cognitive ability. Mixed-effects logistic regression showed that both constructs predicted comprehension accuracy (Experiment 1, Experiment 2). Importantly, the contribution of domain-general cognition was significantly greater for high-ambiguity sentences than similarly constructed sentences containing low-frequency unambiguous words (Experiment 2), showing that effects were not solely attributable to task demands. These findings demonstrate that domain-general cognition functionally contributes to semantic ambiguity resolution and supports core psycholinguistic processes when comprehension demands are high.

articles.title: The Impact of Screen Time and Social Media Use on Adolescent Wellbeing: An Instrumental Variable Analysis
articles.authors: Yi Yang; Jane Maddock; Aaron Kandola; Praveetha Patalay
articles.abstract: Amid worsening mental health of adolescents, the proliferation of digital technologies over the last two decades has attracted particular attention as one potential contributing factor. However, there is a widely cited lack of causal evidence and most prior studies rely on correlational designs vulnerable to reverse causation, unmeasured confounding, and measurement error, while being limited in geographic scope. We estimate the effects of screen time and social media use on adolescent wellbeing using data from 615,133 fifteen-year-olds across 54 countries, applying instrumental variable methods that exploit cross-national and temporal variation in digital environments during the rapid technological diffusion of the mid-2010s. We use year-specific country-level Information and Communication Technology (ICT) usage index and social media penetration rate as instruments generating exogenous variation in adolescents' digital engagement. Instrumental Variables analyses indicate that higher screen time and more social media use caused lower wellbeing in the mid 2010s. The findings were robust to assumption testing and robustness checks. This study contributes multinational causally informed evidence to an empirically contested but policy-relevant debate on the impacts of digital technology on adolescent wellbeing.

articles.title: Stress Beliefs Shape Daily Affective Reactivity to Social and Work Stressors: A 7-Day Ecological Momentary Assessment Study
articles.authors: Johannes Andreas Christoph Laferton; Daniela Conrad; Frank Zimmermann-Viehoff; Stefanie Kunas; Frank Euteneuer; Susanne Fischer; Stefan Salzmann
articles.abstract: Stress beliefs—general expectations about the nature and consequences of stress—have been linked to health and performance, yet their role in shaping responses to everyday stress remains less understood. This study examined whether stress beliefs moderate affective reactivity and stress regulation strategies in response to naturally occurring stressors in daily life. In a 7-day ecological momentary assessment (EMA) study, 104 university students completed thrice-daily reports (1,939 observations) on social and work-related stressors, positive and negative affect, and two stress regulation strategies—situation selection and response modification—as proposed in the Stress Optimization Model (Crum, Jamieson, & Akinola, 2020). At baseline, stress beliefs were assessed alongside variables to characterize the sample. Negative stress beliefs moderated the association between daily stressors and positive affect: individuals with high negative stress beliefs showed reduced positive affect during social stress and no increase in positive affect under higher workload. In contrast, those with low or moderate negative stress beliefs maintained positive affect during social stress and showed increased positive affect under higher workload—consistent with a challenge-like appraisal. Additionally, controllability beliefs buffered increases in negative affect in response to daily social stress, with lower beliefs linked to stronger affective reactivity. In contrast, stress beliefs did not moderate associations between daily stressors and the investigated stress regulation strategies. These findings suggest that stress beliefs tune affective reactivity to everyday social and work stressors, identifying them as a potential intervention target given the established links between affect and long-term health. The generalizability of these findings should be confirmed across broader populations.

articles.title: Developing the Playground Heroes program: An evidence- and theory-based approach to promote positive bystander behavior in racist bullying
articles.authors: Karen Maria Akuah Sieben-Aduful; Giel van Lankveld; Lilian Lechner; Maria Sapouna; Roy A. Willems
articles.abstract:
Background: Racist bullying is a serious societal issue that can have detrimental effects on the health and development of victimized children. To address racist bullying, we developed the Playground Heroes program, aimed at promoting positive bystander behavior targeting pupils and teachers. This article describes the systematic development of this theory and evidence based program. 
Methods: The Playground Heroes program is based on the GATE-BULL intervention and developed using the Intervention Mapping approach. A need assessment was conducted based on relevant literature, focus group discussions with pupils and interviews with teachers to gain more insight into factors that influence bystander behavior. The results led to the development of a program consisting of a lesson plan with a serious game and classroom activities, a teacher training, and a parent brochure. Prior to the start of the lesson plan teachers complete the teacher training about racism and addressing racist bullying. The lesson plan starts with the serious game which is followed by classroom activities matching the scenarios of the game. Primary schools are recruited by cold calling and through online flyers. The evaluation plan concerns a non-randomized controlled trial, consisting of an intervention group and a control group, with one measurement at baseline, and two follow-up measurements at four and eight weeks. 
Discussion: Using the Intervention Mapping approach resulted into the systematic development of a theory and evidence based school program to promote positive bystander behavior in racist bullying.

articles.title: Quantifying the negative impact of gambling: Psychometric evaluation of the Spanish version of the short Gambling Harm Scale (S-SGHS) among individuals engaged in gambling activities
articles.authors: Javier Mora Salgueiro; Guido Corradi; Cristina Martin Perez; José C. Perales; Ivan Blanco; Covadonga Chaves; Carmelo Vazquez; JUAN FRANCISCO NAVAS
articles.abstract: Gambling-related harm (GRH) extends beyond addiction, affecting multiple life domains, yet validated tools for its assessment are currently lacking for the Spanish population. This study adapted and validated a Spanish version of the Gambling Related Harm (GRH) Checklist and developed a short form (S-SGHS) to quantify GRH and facilitate population-level metrics. A Spanish survey of 800 individuals, with gambling experience in the past year, assessed their gambling behaviors, GRH (via the 72-item GRH-CL), the Problem Gambling Severity Index (PGSI), and quality of life (SF-6D, TECVASP). Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) supported a unidimensional structure for the 72-item. Using a genetic algorithm and manual Item Response Theory (IRT) selection, a 12-item short form (S-SGHS-12) was derived, showing strong internal consistency and gender invariance. The S-SGHS demonstrated good predictive validity over the PGSI for quality of life (SF-6D, TECVASP), highlighting its sensitivity to subtle harms. Results underscore the need for culturally tailored instruments. The S-SGHS enables nuanced public health evaluations, informing preventive strategies beyond clinical addiction frameworks. This study provides the first validated Spanish tool for GRH assessment, advancing insights into gambling’s multifaceted impacts.

articles.title: Barriers and facilitators to improving sleep in mental health settings: A mixed-methods systematic review of patient and professional perspectives using the Theoretical Domains Framework
articles.authors: Alexander J Scott; Ciara Kelly; Abigail Locke; Jade Campbell; Susan M Sherman
articles.abstract:
Background: Sleep disturbances are highly prevalent in people with mental health difficulties and are now recognised as a causal, transdiagnostic treatment target. Yet, despite strong evidence, sleep interventions remain underused in routine care. To increase uptake, a comprehensive understanding of the barriers and facilitators is needed to inform the development and implementation of more suitable interventions. 

Method: We conducted a mixed-methods systematic review following PRISMA guidelines. Data were synthesised using a triangulated, convergent integrated approach and mapped to the Theoretical Domains Framework (TDF). 

Results: Nineteen studies (n = 1,988) identified 167 barriers and 141 facilitators across all 14 TDF domains, indicating a complex and multifaceted implementation challenge, with Environmental Context and Resources most prominent (65 barriers, 39%). Within this domain, professionals emphasised service-level constraints (e.g. time, workforce constraints), whereas patients highlighted intervention burden and unsuitable home environments. Beyond this, barriers were most commonly coded to beliefs about capabilities, social influences, beliefs about consequences, and knowledge. Patient and professional accounts differed in emphasis, and we provide a detailed, triangulated synthesis across perspectives and TDF domains, highlighting themes such as low perceived control over sleep, uncertainty around responsibility for sleep, and limited social and organisational support for delivery. 

Conclusions: This review is the first to highlight that barriers to implementing sleep interventions are shaped primarily by environmental context and resources, alongside key psychological determinants of engagement across both patients and professionals. In response, we identify five core principles to guide the development and implementation of sleep interventions in mental health settings.

articles.title: Babble and First Word Learning: The Articulatory Filter Revisited
articles.authors: Marilyn Vihman; Christopher Martin Mikkelsen Cox
articles.abstract: Production and perception of speech develop in tandem during the first year of life, shaping one another in ways that have only recently been put to the experimental test. Canonical babbling, which emerges robustly between 6 and 8 months across languages, is grounded in neuromotor maturation but also depends critically on audition. To account for how production experience might shape word learning, Vihman (1993) proposed an ‘articulatory filter’ that spotlights adult speech patterns matching an infant’s emerging motor routines. Here we trace this proposal to its origins and review the experimental evidence from the past 15 years, much of it using Vocal Motor Schemes (VMS) – frequent, stable consonantal patterns identified from naturalistic recordings – as an index of phonetic mastery. Studies employing VMS demonstrate that infants’ production patterns modulate their attention to and memory for both nonwords and familiar words, and that earlier VMS attainment predicts later lexical and phonological outcomes. Recent acoustic work has begun to validate the VMS construct, while parallel lines of research extend the logic to the pre-canonical period and to infants’ attraction to the sound of infant voices, especially their own. With further validation, VMS may serve as an early clinical indicator of vocabulary delay.

articles.title: Pole-Augmented Multidimensional Scaling Framework for Survival-Analysis Visualization
articles.authors: Takemi Mori
articles.abstract:
We introduce a multidimensional scaling framework that represents survival
outcomes and covariates in a single two-dimensional map. Two reference points called poles
represent the event direction and survival or non-event direction at a fixed follow-up time.
The poles and covariates are represented in a multidimensional scaling space, such that each
covariate is closer to one pole. This representation is intended to combine two readings in a
unified visualization, namely, the relational structure between covariates and each covariate
location relative to the two survival-outcome poles. The event pole is built from the martingale
residual of a null Cox model, while the non-event pole is built from a Kaplan–Meier
pseudo-survival quantity. To produce a clearly interpretable unified visualization, Pearson
