articles.title: От IT-сектора к экосистеме доступа: семислойная рамка online-only исследования цифрового класса
articles.authors: Nikolay Sudnikov
articles.abstract: This article proposes a seven-layer organizational frame for online-only research on the digital class in state-entangled digital states — contexts in which conventional methods (interviews, representative surveys, labor-market statistics) are either unavailable or systematically biased, and where the sectoral optics of “IT firms plus workers” cannot hold the simultaneous weight of state, regulatory, investment, educational, symbolic-mediation, and civic-audit layers. Conceptually, the article proposes a shift from “sector” to “access-conversion ecosystem”: technical capital is converted into product, infrastructural, financial, educational, symbolic, and accountability resources through seven conversion functions (L1–L7). Methodologically, it proposes an online-only sampling frame with an evidence-ladder discipline (SRC/TRC/EVD/LNK/SNP/AMB registers and EV_STRONG/CONF_3 grades) and an explicitly tagged bias-correction supplement that turns visibility bias from a methodological limitation into a coded variable and a diagnostic instrument for the sociology of online visibility. The Kazakhstan case (Pilot 1: 150 core organizations across the seven layers, with 30 bias-correction supplement candidates) reveals four structural observations: the production core is less directly observable than its public periphery; gatekeeping (L4) is asymmetrically centralized; the education pipeline (L5) operates as a promise machine with a gap between the visibility of promise and the visibility of outcome; symbolic mediation (L6) and civic audit (L7) function as independent capitals, irreducible to “industry plus context”. The “visibility ≠ influence” discipline emerges not as a methodological caveat but as substantive defense of mapped actors against the forms of political miscodification typical of state-entangled contexts. The instrument is applicable to parallel digital states with active ecosystem policy and a developed public-visibility infrastructure.

articles.title: Digital Diffusion: Mobile Internet and the Spread of Cultural Scripts about Family Life
articles.authors: Casey Breen; Ridhi Kashyap; Till Koebe
articles.abstract: Global cultural diffusion theories have long emphasized how institutional actors such as intergovernmental organizations, international non-governmental organizations, and state agencies spread cultural scripts. We theorize that mobile internet constitutes a qualitatively distinct pathway for the diffusion of these scripts, shifting exposure from institutionally mediated and episodic to more individualized, continuous, and algorithmically curated. This reconfiguration changes both which cultural models individuals encounter and the frequency with which individuals are exposed to them. Using the staggered rollout of mobile internet across Nigeria as an empirical case, we find that expanding coverage reduces fertility, corresponding to a 9% relative decline in the annual probability of birth, consistent with shifts in contraceptive use, ideal family size, and women’s autonomy in financial and healthcare decision-making. These findings suggest that digital infrastructure does not simply extend existing diffusion processes but can reshape them, with measurable consequences for family life.

articles.title: A Multi-District Markov Chain Monte Carlo Sampler for Redistricting
articles.authors: Christopher T. Kenny
articles.abstract: Random sampling of redistricting plans has become a standard tool for detecting gerrymandering and evaluating proposed maps. Most MCMC redistricting algorithms operate by merging two adjacent districts and splitting them back into two new districts at each step of the chain. I extend this Merge-Split framework with the Multiple Merge Sequential Split (MMSS) algorithm, which generalizes each step to merge ℓ ≥ 2 adjacent districts and repartition them into ℓ new districts via a sequence of ℓ − 1 uniform spanning tree cuts. MMSS targets the same spanning-forest-weighted distribution used by existing samplers, and I derive the Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio. I validate the algorithm by comparing samples against exactly enumerated distributions on small grid maps and compare its scalability with two related algorithms on a realistic redistricting problem. The algorithm supports any value of ℓ ≥ 2, giving users direct control over per-step exploration. MMSS is implemented in the open-source redist R package.

articles.title: Limits of Language Models for Qualitative Coding: Detecting Calls to Action in r/Antiwork
articles.authors: Jessica Witte; Ari Stillman
articles.abstract: Recent studies have demonstrated that large language models (LLMs) can match human experts’ accuracy in qualitatively coding social media data. However, proprietary LLMs accessed through APIs raise concerns related to cost, accessibility, and data protection. Although smaller language models (SLMs) have received less scholarly attention, they can be deployed locally and are open-access, which in turn can mitigate many of the ethical concerns of LLMs. This study evaluates three open-access SLMs in two zero-shot qualitative coding tasks identifying and classifying calls to action (CTAs) within posts from the Reddit community r/Antiwork. Drawing on framing theory and the concept of infrapolitics, we examine how models interpret both collective and individually oriented forms of mobilizing discourse. Across both tasks, models demonstrated consistently low agreement with human-coded ground truth and were least successful at identifying implicit, individual, and infrapolitical calls to action embedded in personal narrative. Qualitative close reading revealed that models most accurately identified CTAs when posts contained explicit directive language and recognizable collective action framing, but were less able to identify CTAs embedded in community-specific and context-dependent forms of mobilization. These findings suggest that zero-shot prompting may be poorly suited to qualitative coding tasks involving implicit social movement discourse. Furthermore, computational approaches to researching mobilization may systematically privilege explicit collective action over individualized or infrapolitical forms of resistance.

articles.title: Reframing biodiversity conservation using social data and artificial intelligence
articles.authors: Oscar Morton; Sebastian Heilpern; Melissa Arias; Sharon Baruch-Mordo; Lucy Cash; Nathan Fox; Annette Hübschle; Koffi Valentin Mawougnigan; Ting Luo; Jacqueline Jürgens
articles.abstract: Human behaviour is central to biodiversity loss, yet conservation rarely harnesses the wealth of social datasets that capture and reflect these behaviours. Here, we argue that integrating diverse social data with recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) can reveal early behavioural signals of emerging threats, enabling a shift from reactive mitigation to anticipatory action. Using illegal wildlife trade mitigation as an illustrative example, we outline how AI-enabled analyses of social data can be applied across the conservation project lifecycle, and highlight key safeguards and priority steps for piloting these approaches in policy-relevant settings.

articles.title: On the New Design Approach for Mountain Villas
articles.authors: 周勇
articles.abstract: Based on the value of mountain villas and the problems they face, this paper proposes a new design approach for mountain villas using electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) aircraft as the primary means of transportation. The advantages of this new type of mountain villa are: 1) No need to construct roads; they can be built on any terrain, even on isolated peaks, allowing residents to stay far away from bustling cities without inconvenience. 2) The scenery and privacy will be of the highest order, offering residents a breathtaking living experience and ultimate enjoyment. These villas will set a new benchmark for luxury estates and are also suitable for high end vacation and leisure development. 3) The environmental impact is within acceptable limits, and at the same time, these villas will add new landscape features to mountainous areas. However, there are also some non negligible issues, such as construction costs, eVTOL traffic safety, seismic and wind resistance of the buildings, and public perception. These issues are discussed in detail.

articles.title: Hans Hass and Jacques-Yves Cousteau. Pioneering Achievements and Structural Inequalities in the Construction of Maritime Memory.
articles.authors: Michael Jung
articles.abstract:
This study examines the parallel yet highly divergent careers of Hans Hass and Jacques-Yves Cousteau, two central pioneers of twentieth century underwater exploration. While both contributed substantially to marine science, diving technology, and the visual mediation of the underwater world, their long-term global recognition developed in markedly different ways. Drawing on diverse interdisciplinary perspectives, the article explores how geopolitical conditions, institutional support systems, narrative self-presentation, and the rise of a global media ecology shaped their public visibility.
Beyond a biographical or technical comparison, the juxtaposition of Hass and Cousteau is deliberately employed as a conceptual case study to examine the formation of maritime memory in the twentieth century underwater research.
The findings demonstrate that Cousteau’s worldwide prominence resulted not solely from scientific or technological achievement, but from his exceptional integration into powerful institutional, technological, and cultural infrastructures that amplified and internationalized his work. In contrast, Hass—despite significant early innovation and ecological awareness—was constrained by weaker institutional embeddedness, a scientifically restrained and emotionally neutral serial television aesthetic with limited long-term repeatability, and subsequent theoretical isolation.
A decisive factor in this divergence was Cousteau’s entrepreneurial engagement with emerging television markets, which transformed underwater exploration into a scalable media enterprise and provided the structural basis for his enduring visibility.
Overall, the analysis suggests that maritime memory is shaped less by scientific merit alone than by the ways media, politics, and economic scalability stabilize certain cultural narratives. These dynamics have produced asymmetrical legacies that continue to shape how ocean exploration is remembered and imagined today.

articles.title: Negotiating the Good Mother Ideal: Maternal Guilt in Contemporary Society
articles.authors: Maria-Valentina Stoicescu; Natalia Soboleva; Nevena Kulic; Giulia Dotti Sani
articles.abstract: This narrative review integrates quantitative and qualitative research to provide a comprehensive overview of the phenomenon of maternal guilt, examining its measurement, types, triggers, and prevalence. Maternal guilt is a widespread emotion that is associated with the societal role of women as caregivers, yet this association is sometimes lost when talking more broadly about parental guilt. Critical gaps persist in the understanding and measurement of maternal guilt, particularly in the areas of cross-cultural variation, intersectional perspectives, and the long-term consequences for both mothers and their children. The classification of maternal guilt into types highlights that maternal guilt can take different forms, depending on specific root causes and circumstances. By analysing triggers, this review also shows that maternal guilt is enhanced through individual, situational, and institutional factors, and it varies across countries, being influenced by societal and cultural characteristics.

articles.title: Locally-Driven Developmental United Front Work: County-Level Mobilization in China's Rural Revitalization
articles.authors: Yi Duan
articles.abstract: This article develops the concept of "locally-driven developmental united front work" to explain how county-level united front departments in China have moved beyond traditional political coordination and elite co-optation toward active involvement in economic growth, cultural tourism, and social governance. Drawing on participant observation and archival analysis of Shangyou County (Jiangxi Province) between 2022 and 2025, the study identifies four mechanisms through which united front resources are transformed into developmental outcomes: resource capitalization, platform repurposing, institutional bricolage, and cross-sector brokerage. The analysis focuses on five empirical domains: cultural tourism integration, private-sector innovation, non-Party cadre cultivation, grassroots organizational innovation, and digital capacity building. The findings suggest that under conditions of fiscal constraint and strong upper-level policy support, county-level united front leaders can function as developmental entrepreneurs who recombine existing institutional materials to generate locally tailored growth strategies. This reconceptualization extends the literature on China’s united front system and contributes to broader debates on local state developmentalism and institutional entrepreneurship.

articles.title: Governance causality theory: A causal architecture for regulatory and administrative systems
articles.authors: Michael McGuinness
articles.abstract: This theoretical manuscript develops a causal architecture for governance and administrative systems, explaining how authority, regulation, and institutional design interact to produce administrative outcomes. It forms part of a thesis‑by‑publication on governance architecture and delegated authority. The paper proposes a structural model linking causal mechanisms of rule formation, execution‑time authority, and institutional adaptation within public administration.

articles.title: Using Statistical Modelling and Geographic Data to Challenge Declinist Headlines - a Case Study of Fish and Chip Shops in Great Britain
articles.authors: Stephen Clark; Andy Newing
articles.abstract: The traditional fish and chip shop is an important part of the food environment in many UK neighbourhoods. However, rising costs, changing tastes and competition have resulted in fish and chip shop closures, with some alarmist predictions that up to half of all shops could close in a single year. Using Points of Interest data and multilevel modelling, we look beyond the headlines and model trends in fish and chip shop provision by neighbourhood type between 2022 and 2025. We identify an overall decline in the number of shops yet find no evidence to support the predictions of widespread closures. However, closures have been unevenly spread across neighbourhood types, with the greatest decline in deprived and ethnically diverse urban areas, and less impact on some more affluent neighbourhoods with older populations. Although some adaption may be needed to maintain viable amidst rising costs, our findings suggest that the traditional fish and chip shop remains an important part of the food environment in many neighbourhoods. In conclusion, we highlight the importance of robust data driven insight when considering the changing provision of services and comment on how these approaches could be applied in other contexts.

articles.title: Exploring the determinants of men’s involvement in group walking: A demonstrative network modelling study.
articles.authors: Brendan John Smith; Eleanor Quested; Joanne A McVeigh; Matthew McDonald; Dominika Kwasnicka; Janelle Woods; Elizabeth Calleja; Hugh Riddell
articles.abstract:
Background: Men in Australia are more likely than women to present with risk factors for coronary heart disease and are underrepresented in community-based physical activity (PA) initiatives. Understanding the characteristics of those who do participate can help identify groups not being reached. Traditional statistical approaches may not capture the complex interrelationships between demographic and behavioural factors that shape participation. This exploratory study demonstrates the utility of network modelling for public health research by identifying characteristics associated with men’s involvement in The National Heart Foundation Australia’s Heart Foundation Walking (HFW) groups program and examining how these characteristics interrelate.
Methods: Exploratory network modelling was conducted using complete data from 30,909 participants (19.3% male; mean age=63.4, SD=14.38, range=18-96years). Variables included demographics (age, gender, socioeconomic status), location, enrolment year, role (organiser vs member) and self-reported PA at enrolment. Conditional dependencies among these variables were modelled to identify patterns associated with male participation.
Results: Compared to women, men at enrolment were older (mean age=65 vs 63), self-reported meeting PA guidelines (44% vs 37%), and were more often organisers (2.3% vs 1.7%). Role was the most central variable, showing the strongest associations with others, while gender had lower centrality and was positioned peripherally. Self-report PA acted as a bridge node connecting characteristics.
Conclusions: Network analysis visualised complex relationships among participant characteristics, revealing patterns not captured by traditional approaches. This method provides a tool for generating hypotheses about participation in community-based health initiatives. Further research should explore men’s experiences in HFW-groups to guide practical strategies for engagement.

articles.title: Disentangling Repetition and Imitation Components of Preferential Attachment in Social Action Networks
articles.authors: C. Ben Gibson
articles.abstract: Models of social action networks frequently find that preferential attachment -- the recurring pattern in which individuals or entities that already have many social ties tend to attract even more over time -- is a powerful predictor of social interaction over time. The most common explanation of preferential attachment is social imitation, i.e., selecting interaction targets based on how others have selected targets in the past. However, the most commonly used measure of preferential attachment, target indegree, conflates two distinct mechanisms: social imitation, in which actors mimic behaviors they observe in others, and self-repetition, in which actors repeat their own past behaviors. Drawing from classical sociological theory, this paper proposes a conceptual and empirical distinction between these processes and demonstrates that they exhibit different sensitivities to features of the interaction environment. Using a hierarchical relational event model across a corpus of hundreds of social action networks, we show that models separating imitation and repetition provide improved fit and more interpretable results. In addition, while social imitation varies systematically with interaction visibility in the social ecology, repetition remains significant and positive when interaction visibility is low. The findings clarify the behavioral foundations of preferential attachment and provide conceptual precision in modeling sequential social dynamics across social ecologies.

articles.title: Toward a Generative Theory of Social Structure: How Social Feedback and Reputation Produce Norms, Power, and Meaning
articles.authors: Peter Marbach
articles.abstract:
How do behavioral norms stabilize, shared interpretive frameworks consolidate, and power hierarchies form and reproduce — all without central coordination, prior agreement, or external enforcement, and all from the ordinary dynamics of everyday interaction? This paper presents a generative answer: a single recursive feedback mechanism, formalized as the socially responsive agent (SRA) model, from which all of these structures emerge endogenously, without external enforcement, pre-existing normative orders, or tradition-specific auxiliary assumptions.
The mechanism is compact. Agents monitor symbolically mediated social feedback and adapt their behavior to increase their reputational standing within a reference group. The iterated operation of this loop, extended by gossip networks and imitation of opinion leaders, generates behavioral norms, shared schemas, and stable power hierarchies as natural consequences rather than as independent phenomena. These structures are unified within the concept of the social reward economy, which produces cohesion, polarization, resistance, and manipulation as structural outcomes of the same feedback dynamics.
The theoretical stakes extend further. Mapped onto the SRA mechanism, the core contributions of Mead, Giddens, Bourdieu, Habermas, Luhmann, and Coleman appear not as incommensurable rivals but as accounts of different components of the same generative process. Most consequentially, the Habermas–Luhmann debate — long treated as an irresolvable confrontation between incompatible social ontologies — turns out to describe two empirically distinct regimes of the same underlying mechanism: one prevailing before opinion leadership consolidates, one after. What appeared philosophical becomes an empirical question. The paper develops this argument as a unified whole, drawing on work developed in its companion papers.

articles.title: Healing Beyond the Table: The Role of Trauma and Gender in Mediation
articles.authors: Sam Chaaban
articles.abstract: Peace negotiations frequently collapse not because of failed diplomacy, but because of unaddressed trauma and the systematic exclusion of women. This policy analysis examines two critical dimensions of sustainable peacebuilding — trauma-informed practice and gender-inclusive participation — through comparative case studies of Rwanda and South Sudan. Rwanda's Gacaca courts illustrate both the healing potential and the re-traumatization risks of community-based truth-telling, while South Sudan's repeated peace failures expose the costs of excluding women and ignoring psychosocial wounds. Drawing on the Women, Peace and Security (WPS) framework anchored in UN Security Council Resolution 1325, this piece argues that durable peace requires mediation processes that are simultaneously trauma-conscious and gender-sensitive. Five concrete recommendations are offered for mediators, policymakers, and international organizations.

articles.title: Lexical Thresholds in Medical English II: AI-Assisted Text Simplification and Its Reletionship on Vocabulary and Reading Comprehension
articles.authors: Evgeni Stanchev
articles.abstract:
This study explores how AI-assisted text simplification may influence the relationship between medical vocabulary knowledge and reading comprehension in English for Medical Purposes (EMP). Building on a previous phase, the study adopts a two-group design within a single cohort of 84 second-year medical students, comparing performance on original and simplified versions of the same biochemistry texts. The simplified texts were generated using ChatGPT. Participants completed a 45-item test consisting of 25 vocabulary recognition items and 20 reading comprehension questions. The analysis focuses on overall performance, the relationship between lexical knowledge and comprehension, and the extent to which learners appear to transfer vocabulary knowledge to reading tasks.
The results suggest generally high levels of performance in the AI-assisted condition, with mean scores of 91% for vocabulary and 84% for comprehension. A positive relationship was observed between vocabulary knowledge and comprehension (r = .74). When compared with the original-text condition (r = .56), this pattern may indicate a somewhat stronger alignment between lexical knowledge and comprehension under simplified conditions. Transfer-efficiency results (M = 0.92) suggest that students were generally able to apply their lexical knowledge in reading, although with notable individual variation. At the same time, differences in performance between texts remained, suggesting that text-related factors may continue to play a role even under simplified conditions.

articles.title: Hardwarism and biofoundry imaginaries in South Korea
articles.authors: Joonhyung Bae; Dae-hee Lee
articles.abstract: International proponent literature legitimates biofoundries (the automated infrastructure of synthetic biology) through democratization rhetoric. That register has bifurcated since 2015 and faced a parallel strategic-asset register since 2022. How Korean stakeholders who shape biofoundry governance position themselves in this fragmenting register has not been measured. A fifteen-expert Korean panel evaluated four candidate framings against an anchoring interview with a senior researcher at a national biofoundry centre. A four-corpus parallel inspection (Korean policy texts, international policy and proponent-community documents, outward-facing Korean publications, civic-bioethics archive material across three sub-strata) and a Singapore illustrative vignette converge on the panel finding. Among the four offered framings, eight of fifteen mark bio-democratization most problematic while eleven endorse the instrumentalist bio-factory framing as most useful. The democratization lexicon appears in Korean civic-society discourse but disappears in statutory and biofoundry-policy registers, and every panelist-articulated alternative preserves the producer-consumer schema rather than the co-producer alternative. We read the pattern as consistent with Kim's (2011) hardwarism, the Korean discursive structure that treats technology as hardware produced by experts and adopted by users, a reading licensed for cross-national testing rather than within-panel proof. The Korean case sits in a developmental-state pathway, distinct from the UK pathway of attenuated democratization framings and from the post-2022 international pivot to strategic-asset framing. The paper contributes a within-group check protocol for stakeholder-consultation design and a policy diagnosis. Democratization vocabulary will not deliver the participatory legitimacy it promises, whereas an accountability-and-interoperability vocabulary aligned with the bio-factory framing does more analytical work.

articles.title: Systematic Evaluation of Nation-State Propaganda in LLM Outputs
articles.authors: Kevin T. Greene; Jacob N Shapiro
articles.abstract: The rapid growth and usability of AI is creating new risks to information quality, including the possibility that chatbot responses spread propaganda from state-backed influence campaigns. There is little systematic evidence regarding how frequently LLMs spread such narratives when responding to user queries. Previous attempts to assess these risks rely on small numbers of evaluation questions, use opaque query construction procedures, and fail to account for key sources of uncertainty in model responses. We address these gaps with an automated, reproducible pipeline that samples documented state-backed propaganda and news items, programmatically generates evaluation questions, and audits LLM-generated responses. We evaluate whether five prominent models: (1) reproduce arguments aligned with documented Kremlin propaganda narratives; (2) present the Kremlin perspective on events without providing corrective context; and (3) cite known propaganda outlets. We find substantially lower rates of propaganda-aligned responses and propaganda-source citation than prior small-sample work would suggest. These risks are particularly rare for questions resembling ordinary information searches about Ukraine. Our approach enables systematic comparisons across models and issue areas, supports ongoing monitoring, and could be extended to many other hotly-debated topics.

articles.title: Navigating the Fractured Frontier: Legal Risks in Vietnam’s International Financial Centers and Strategic Pathways for Global and Vietnam Investors
articles.authors: Son Nguyen
articles.abstract: Vietnam’s International Financial Centers (IFCs) in Ho Chi Minh City and Danang represent a strategic pivot toward deep integration into the global financial architecture. Established under Resolution 222/2025/QH15, these centers promise flexible legal frameworks, preferential tax regimes, and specialized dispute resolution mechanisms. Yet, implementation reveals both visible and hidden legal risks that can undermine predictability and investment efficacy. Employing doctrinal legal analysis, comparative institutional benchmarking, and contrarian critique, this paper dissects two risk strata: visible risks (ambiguous legal status, fragmented governance, foreign exchange controls, and AML/CFT gaps) and hidden risks (enforcement discretion, judicial conservatism, cross-border data sovereignty conflicts, and geopolitical friction). Challenging the traditional “legal enclave” paradigm, the study argues that IFC success depends on systemic alignment with Vietnam’s domestic legal ecosystem rather than institutional isolation. The paper proposes a strategic architecture encompassing hybrid dispute resolution, dynamic contracting with stabilization clauses, co-regulatory dialogue, and climate-aligned ESG compliance. Its primary contribution lies in a multi-layered legal risk taxonomy and a paradigm shift from risk mitigation toward adaptive institutional co-creation, offering actionable frameworks for multinational enterprises, legal practitioners, and policymakers navigating emerging market financial hubs.

articles.title: Teaching Teachers
articles.authors: Danilo Kuzmanic; Per Engzell; John Jerrim
articles.abstract: Good teachers are role models. Using Chilean administrative data, we estimate how teacher value-added (VA) affects the VA of students who later become teachers. Exposure to a math teacher with 1 SD higher VA raises teacher VA in the next generation by 0.14 SD and student achievement one generation removed by 0.04 SD. Effects compound with longer exposure and appear driven by observable classroom practices. Under plausible assumptions, these spillovers raise cumulative returns to instructional quality by 28 percent after one generation and 39 percent in the long run.

articles.title: Is Peaceful Resistance Really Better?
articles.authors: Charles Crabtree; John B. Holbein
articles.abstract: Are peaceful social movements really more effective than violent ones? Chenoweth and Stephan's Why Civil Resistance Works argues that nonviolent resistance consistently outperforms violent resistance in achieving democratic outcomes. This conclusion has had enormous influence among academics, political actors, the media, and on-the-ground activists. We find that some results were misreported in ways that make the data appear to support civil resistance when they do not. Moreover, we find that the results are not robust to modest and reasonable changes in model specification, that they are sensitive to omitted variable bias, and that they rely on instrumental variable models with implausible assumptions that yield unstable estimates. Taken together, our replication and extension suggest that the available cross-national evidence provides no clear basis for the claim that nonviolent campaigns are substantially more effective than violent ones. This is not evidence that nonviolence backfires; it is evidence that the strong, unconditional advantage reported in Why Civil Resistance Works is not supported by the data. Our findings underscore the need for methodological re-examinations of foundational works.

articles.title: Peace, War, and the Spirit of Commerce: Genovesi as a reader of Montesquieu
articles.authors: JHET Assistant; Paolo Santori
articles.abstract: What are the consequences of trade among nations? In De l’esprit des lois, Montesquieu claimed that the spirit of commerce fosters peace, restraining conflict by leading nations to collaborate in pursuit of mutual benefit. Economic interests, he suggested, channel and moderate the violent impulses of human passions. In contrast, the Neapolitan philosopher and economist Antonio Genovesi offered a strikingly different interpretation. In his reading of Montesquieu, Genovesi argued that the spirit of commerce was itself a spirit of conquest—an engine of rivalry and domination among nations. Yet, he maintained, the telos of trade rests upon an alternative spirit, one of mutual aid, capable of nurturing peace and cooperation both within and between societies. This paper offers a philosophical analysis of the dialogue between Montesquieu and Genovesi, tracing how their exchange shaped Genovesi’s evolving reflections on the intricate relationship between commerce and war.

articles.title: Trade Integration, Family Formation, and the 'Korean' Deaths of Despair: Evidence from Sigungu-Level Bilateral Exposure
articles.authors: Jae Heon Jung
articles.abstract: South Korea's bilateral trade integration with China during the 2000s offers a natural setting to study whether trade exposure raises or lowers local mortality in an export-oriented economy. Using a shift-share instrumental variable on a panel of 221 Korean districts, I document a protective effect of trade exposure on working-age deaths of despair: a one standard deviation increase reduces the long-difference change in age-standardized mortality by β = −0.127, structurally opposite to the adverse findings for the United States and the United Kingdom. The estimate retains about half of its magnitude (β = −0.071) after controlling for cross-district modernization. The protective effect is concentrated on the deaths-of-despair composite under family-wise error control; cancer, cardiovascular, respiratory, and external-other mortality show no response. A joint mediator decomposition attributes 59 percent of the effect to marriage-market and family-formation channels, in contrast to the labor-market and opioid pathways that dominate the U.S. case.

articles.title: Industry Differences in Upward Mobility from Low-Wage Labour Market Entry
articles.authors: Karin Kristensson
articles.abstract: A low-wage occupation is a common entry point into the labour market, yet the trajectories that follow vary considerably. This study examines whether patterns of upward mobility from low-wage labour market entry differ across industries. Using Swedish full population register data, the analysis follows all individuals aged 18–25 who entered the labour market in 2003 in low wage occupations and traces their occupational trajectories over 18 years. Event history models are used to estimate the timing of transitions from low wage to higher wage occupations. The results indicate substantial and persistent differences across industries in both the pace and likelihood of upward mobility. Workers in manufacturing and construction tend to move into higher wage occupations more quickly and more often, whereas those in hospitality, cleaning, and personal services experience markedly slower transitions. In several of these service industries, workers remain in low wage occupations more than twice as long as comparable workers in manufacturing, and a sizeable share do not transition at all within the observation period. As these service industries expand their share of low wage jobs—and as women and immigrants remain disproportionately concentrated within them—these patterns raise concerns about social stratification. The findings highlight how industrial structures shape early career mobility and interact with broader labour market inequalities, underscoring the importance of industrial opportunity structures for understanding trajectories from low-wage work.

articles.title: Reconsidering the Relationship Between Intelligence and Right-Wing Authoritarianism and Social Dominance Orientation: Evidence from a Discordant Twin Analysis
articles.authors: Mirko Ruks; Christian Kandler; Jana Instinske; Edward Bell
articles.abstract: Previous research has consistently shown that higher intelligence (IQ) is linked to lower levels of Right-Wing Authoritarianism (RWA) and Social Dominance Orientation (SDO). However, prior studies have been largely correlational, leaving it unclear to what degree these associations are driven by unobserved confounding. This study addresses this gap using longitudinal data from 6144 twins in the German TwinLife survey and employing a discordant-twin design. We estimated latent-variable within--between models, which allowed us to estimate the effect of IQ on RWA and SDO four years later while accounting for unobserved genetic and familial confounding. The pooled results replicate the negative associations reported in prior work, but the within-family estimates using identical twins show that these associations are substantially inflated by unobserved confounding. Specifically, the link between IQ and RWA appears to be partly driven by common genetic influences, whereas the association between IQ and SDO is primarily confounded by shared environmental factors. These findings suggest that the role of cognitive ability in fostering more liberal attitudes is often overstated and underscore the value of genetically informed designs in political psychology.

articles.title: La paradoja del bienestar emprendedor: una revisión narrativa sobre autonomía, estrés y desempeño
articles.authors: cristian hernández toro
articles.abstract:
El bienestar emprendedor se ha consolidado como un campo relevante para comprender el emprendimiento más allá de sus resultados económicos. Esta revisión narrativa sintetiza literatura científica publicada hasta el 31 de diciembre de 2023 sobre la relación entre emprendimiento, bienestar psicológico, estrés, desempeño, persistencia y sostenibilidad del negocio. El argumento central es que el emprendimiento produce una paradoja de bienestar: puede ofrecer autonomía, propósito, aprendizaje, identidad y satisfacción, pero también generar incertidumbre financiera, sobrecarga laboral, conflicto vida-trabajo, miedo al fracaso, agotamiento y deterioro de la salud mental. La revisión organiza la literatura en tres partes. Primero, se presentan los fundamentos conceptuales del bienestar emprendedor, integrando bienestar subjetivo, bienestar eudaimónico, salud mental, satisfacción laboral y equilibrio vida-trabajo. Segundo, se analizan factores que fortalecen o deterioran el bienestar, incluyendo autoeficacia, resiliencia, capital psicológico, pasión, apoyo familiar, redes, demandas laborales, género y contexto institucional. Tercero, se examinan las consecuencias del bienestar sobre desempeño, innovación, toma de decisiones, persistencia, salida del negocio, fracaso y recuperación. Se propone un modelo conceptual que entiende el bienestar emprendedor como resultado dinámico de la interacción entre demandas, recursos, mecanismos psicológicos, resultados empresariales y moderadores contextuales. El artículo concluye que el bienestar debe considerarse una dimensión central de la sostenibilidad emprendedora, especialmente en contextos latinoamericanos donde la informalidad, la desigualdad, el emprendimiento por necesidad y la limitada protección social pueden intensificar la vulnerabilidad de quienes emprenden.

Palabras clave: bienestar emprendedor; emprendimiento; estrés; salud mental; desempeño; revisión narrativa.

articles.title: Moral Regulation in Sequential Decisions
articles.authors: Umberto Galmarini; Astrid Gamba; Ibai Martínez-Macías
articles.abstract:
Do individuals become more generous after harming others, or less generous after doing the right thing? We study whether moral behavior spills over across sequential decisions through moral accounting: individuals may offset prior moral debts through subsequent prosocial behavior (moral cleansing) or draw on prior moral credits to justify lower generosity (moral licensing).
In an online experiment, participants first make a fair or unfair allocation in a Dictator Minigame. They then learn whether the Receiver’s payoff was determined by their own choice or by chance, and make an unanticipated decision about whether to donate part of their earnings to a charity. By varying responsibility for realized social outcomes, the design generates different moral states associated with the same first-stage choice, which can trigger compensatory behavior in the subsequent donation decision. 
We find a sharp asymmetry. After choosing the fair allocation, being responsible for the Receiver’s favorable outcome significantly reduces subsequent donations, consistent with moral licensing. After choosing the unfair allocation, responsibility has no average effect on giving, but this null effect conceals substantial heterogeneity in individual responses. Overall, the results show that responsibility for outcomes can shape later prosocial behavior, but does so asymmetrically across good and bad deeds.

articles.title: Who’s afraid of relativism?
articles.authors: John Nelson
articles.abstract: Speakers on both the political right and left often suggest that their opponents are engaged in, or given aid and comfort by, “relativism” or “postmodernism,” a rejection of universal standards of evidence, reason, or morality.  Such rejection is taken to result in a collapse of norms into nihilism, reducing politics to brute propaganda and power play.  Drawing on the philosophical tradition of American pragmatism, I argue that “relativism” is a boogeyman fear, and that rejection of the idea of universal standards does not make discourse, argument, or politics any less based in evidence, reason, or morality than they already were.  I suggest that both accusations of relativism and their corollary claims of transcendent, universal justification are unproductive, overheated distractions from the difficult and pressing work of democratic deliberation, negotiation, and compromise.

articles.title: The Logical Limits of Bias Analysis in Scientific Inquiry
articles.authors: Gang Xie
articles.abstract: Bias analysis is widely discussed in statistics as the expected deviation of an estimator from the true parameter, commonly expressed as Bias(θ ̂ )=E[θ ̂ ]-θ. While mathematically rigorous within fully specified models, this definition becomes logically problematic in empirical science, where θ represents truth and is precisely the quantity under investigation. This short article clarifies why traditional bias analysis cannot meaningfully quantify reality, distinguishes it from legitimate sensitivity analysis, and situates it appropriately within the broader framework of scientific reasoning.

articles.title: Between the Embedding and the Prompt: Systematic Design Effects in LLM-based Occupation Coding
articles.authors: Olga Kononykhina; Anna-Carolina Haensch; Frauke Kreuter
articles.abstract: Assigning free-text job descriptions to standardised taxonomies is a persistent bottleneck in survey research and official statistics. Large language models (LLMs) offer a promising path toward automation, but each step in the pipeline involves both model architecture and measurement choices about how an occupation should be represented. Through 119 experiments on German survey data, we systematically vary the textual representation of occupational categories, embedding models, LLMs, and prompt design. Category representation changes retrieval accuracy by 8–23 percentage points and classification by 11–21. Prompt role and abstention behaviour are model-specific and must be validated before deployment. The dominant source of variance, however, sits outside model measurement choices. How respondents describe their work matters more than any model or design choice (ICC = 0.76).

articles.title: TOWARD MORPHOLOGICALLY-AWARE TOKENIZATION FOR INFLECTIONAL LANGUAGES: A STRUCTURAL HYPOTHESIS
articles.authors: Elżbieta Dawidek
articles.abstract:
This article proposes a theoretical hypothesis concerning the structural and temporal architecture of tokenization in inflectional languages. The starting point is an observation documented in prior empirical work (Dawidek, 2026a; 2026b): standard frequency-based tokenization systematically fails to preserve morphologically significant boundaries in Polish, a synthetic inflectional language. The present article argues that this failure has a deeper cause than training data composition or algorithm choice. Standard tokenization has no preconditions — it begins cutting without structural knowledge of the word — and no concept of time: it treats the interval between cuts as empty latency rather than as a potential carrier of morphological information.
I propose two interconnected hypotheses. First, that linguistically motivated tokenization requires a morphological skeleton as a precondition — a minimal representation of root, prefix, ending type, case, number, gender, and valency, specified here for Polish and hypothesized to generalize to other inflectional languages. Second, that the cutting order itself is morphologically motivated and temporally structured: prefix first, inflectional ending second, root exposed last — where exposure is not a cut but a revelation of what was present throughout.
I further propose the concept of morphological resolution time — the interval between cuts understood as a site of grammatical resolution rather than computational waste. This proposal has architectural consequences: the temporal dimension required by the hypothesis is structurally incompatible with standard transformer parallelism. A hybrid architecture combining sequential morphological pre-processing with parallel downstream processing is identified as a research direction.
This article presents a hypothesis, not demonstrated results. It is offered as a theoretical framework for empirical work that has not yet been done — and as a named set of variables that must exist before measurement can begin.

articles.title: Mind the Self-Promotion Gap: Gendered Perceptions and Responses to an Informational Intervention
articles.authors: Maalvika Bhat; Inna Smirnova; Misha Teplitskiy; Agnes Horvat
articles.abstract: Promoting one’s research on social media has become an increasingly important part of academic careers, with implications for career advancement and the circulation of scientific knowledge. Prior research finds that men tend to self-promote their research papers more than women. This study draws on a survey of 3,111 U.S.-based academics to examine why this gender gap persists and experimentally test whether informing participants of the gap influences their intentions to self-promote in the future. We find that women are more likely than men to report reputational benefits (e.g., recognition, networking opportunities) but also higher interpersonal and professional costs (e.g., harassment, credibility loss, and time constraints). Men, by contrast, more often report skepticism about usefulness or audience presence as reasons for not self-promoting. Exposure to information about the gender gap is associated with significantly lower stated intentions among men to promote their next paper on social media. Women’s intentions, by contrast, remain unchanged, consistent with perceived costs of self-promotion, not lack of information about the gap, being the primary barrier among women. Overall, we conclude that increasing women’s intentions to self-promote their research requires systemic changes to evaluation criteria, anti-harassment infrastructure, and institutional support for public engagement.

articles.title: The Public Health Impact of GLP-1 Advertising Exposure in the U.S.
articles.authors: Matt Motta; Timothy Callaghan; Sarah K Lipson; Jennifer Ross; Kiersten Strombotne; Christopher Louis
articles.abstract: The recent discovery that a class of drugs collectively known as “GLP-1s” (Glucagon-Like Peptide-1) can promote weight loss has led to a surge in pharmaceutical production, demand, and advertising. While previous research has considered the impact that advertising exposure may have on public demand for using GLP-1s for weight management, few have considered the broader public health impacts (both positive and negative) of GLP-1 advertising. For example, increased GLP-1 advertisement exposure could make people more self-conscious about their own weight and physical appearance. It could also promote destigmatizing views toward those who use GLP-1 products for weight management. In a nationally representative survey of N = 1,000 US adults, we find that people exposed to higher levels of GLP-1 advertising tend to hold more negative views about their own weight and physical appearance. We find no evidence that ad exposure is associated with destigmatizing views about those who use GLP-1 products, although we find strong evidence that exposure can stimulate demand for GLP-1 products. These results hold across a wide range of measures and model specifications. We conclude by discussing the potential public health risks of GLP-1 advertising, in a changing regulatory landscape.

articles.title: Social Entropy, Adaptive Coherence, and Transcendent Detachment: An Integrative Framework for Measuring Coherence and Fragility in Human Systems
articles.authors: Sherif Rashwan
articles.abstract:
Human groups, institutions, relationships, and identities can be modeled as dynamic information-processing systems that require continual maintenance to pre
serve coherence under uncertainty. This article develops Adaptive Coherence and Entropic Attachment (ACEA), an integrative framework linking information theory, cybernetics, resilience science, attachment theory, stress appraisal, predictive processing, allostasis, discourse analysis, and institutional theory. The framework formalizes social coherence as a time-dependent state variable shaped by trust, shared knowledge, relational cohesion, communication clarity, adaptive capacity, regenerative repair, informational noise, fear, uncertainty, external disturbance, and attachment rigidity. The central contribution is a testable account of “transcendent detachment” as adaptive non-rigidity: the capacity to preserve meaning, commitment, and identity without requiring absolute permanence or total control. ACEA is not presented as a finished empirical law, but as a formal research program that generates measurable constructs, falsifiable hypotheses, and practical applications for discourse analysis, organizational resilience, conflict mediation, institutional design, and psychological adaptation.

articles.title: The Origin of Patriarchal Moral System: An Evolutionary Model
articles.authors: Maksim Sapozhnikov
articles.abstract:
[This is a new submission replacing a previous version that was accidentally deleted. The earlier submission was declined due to an incomplete ORCID profile; the profile has now been updated with full professional information, qualifications, and biography. The manuscript is currently under peer review at the journal Human Nature and is not AI-generated.]

This article proposes an evolutionary-materialist model for the origin of the patriarchal moral system — a set of normative prohibitions restricting divorce, female sexuality, and freedom of mate choice. The model takes as its starting point the reproductive strategy of male provisioning — the exchange of male‑provided resources for female reproductive access — shaped by sexual selection. It is shown that the transition from foraging to civilization generates three demographic problems that make this strategy unstable: (a) increased population density facilitating partner switching, (b) awareness of the causal link between intercourse and childbirth reducing female motivation for sex, and (c) dissociation between the timing of sexual and social maturity in males. The normative solution to each of these problems—and their combination as a patriarchal moral system—is proposed to be understood as an adaptation to new demographic conditions. The model explains the convergent emergence of similar prohibitions across different cultures and the specific role of older males in enforcing them. The article is theoretical in nature and can serve as a foundation for further empirical research.

articles.title: Naming under constraint: gender, care and symbolic power in family life
articles.authors: Patrícia Albergaria Almeida
articles.abstract:
This article proposes a reconceptualisation of children’s surname attribution as a gendered site of symbolic power within heterosexual family life. While naming is commonly framed as a private and consensual decision, feminist scholarship has shown that family practices are structured by persistent asymmetries of care, authority, and recognition. Yet the symbolic dimension of naming remains underexamined.
Building on feminist theories of care, symbolic violence, and situated knowledge, this article introduces the concept of naming under constraint to analyse how naming decisions are produced under conditions that limit deliberation and agency. Drawing on a case in Portugal, it shows how a naming decision taken immediately after childbirth - under bodily vulnerability, institutional urgency, and unequal care relations - becomes stabilised as a consensual outcome.
The article further develops the notion of delayed articulation to theorise the temporal gap between living inequality and naming it as such, highlighting how care-intensive life stages constrain feminist critique.
By shifting attention from naming as a neutral outcome to naming as a situated process, this article contributes to sociological debates on gender, care, and symbolic power, and highlights the importance of attending to the temporal and relational conditions under which inequality becomes intelligible.

articles.title: Acceptance of transport policies:  A factorial survey experiment in  the German Longitudinal  Environmental Study (GLEN)
articles.authors: Christiane Bozoyan; Claudia Schmiedeberg; Henning Best
articles.abstract: Due to the significant negative externalities associated with the transport sector, policies that effectively address these issues and guide the transition to a low-carbon transport system are required. In order to gain insight into the level of public support for a number of transport policy interventions currently under debate in Germany, a factorial survey experiment on interventions to promote the transition from combustion engine vehicles to battery electric vehicles (BEVs) was conducted as part of the GLEN starter survey in January/February 2025. This report documents the experiment.

articles.title: A New Approach to Addressing the Rampant Gun Violence in the United States
articles.authors: 周勇
articles.abstract: A ban on ammunition would prohibit the private possession and circulation of ammunition and specialized components used in its manufacture, while allowing government-authorized and regulated commercial shooting ranges and hunting outfitters to possess ammunition and conduct lawful shooting activities. It would also permit the private collection of certified top-tier antique ammunition, and permits reloading enthusiasts to reload ammunition in government-authorized and regulated reloading workshops, while providing a service to deliver the reloaded ammunition to their designated commercial shooting ranges or hunting outfitters. The government subsidizes commercial shooting ranges, hunting outfitters, and reloading workshops, enabling them to offer their primary services free of charge to consumers. The new policy implements robust regulatory measures for ammunition in civilian circulation, and repeals all existing gun control laws and regulations, allowing anyone to freely own and carry firearms, and explicitly states that no background checks will be conducted on consumers entering commercial shooting ranges, hunting outfitters, or reloading workshops. The new policy has a minimal impact on the firearms industry’s revenue (8%) and does not increase the government’s fiscal burden (annual subsidies account for 0.2% of federal revenue). The new policy significantly reduces the cost of sport shooting (by 26%) and high-end hunting (by 9%), while causing virtually no harm to the public’s shooting rights (affected shooting activities account for only 10% of those prior to the policy). The new policy benefits 24% of all adults, while only 4% of adults are negatively affected, and will pass smoothly in both the House and Senate with approximately 85% support. This widespread public support will prompt the Supreme Court to issue a new judicial interpretation of the Second Amendment, ensuring the smooth implementation of the new policy.

articles.title: Panel Study of Russian Public Opinion and Attitudes (PROPA) Wave 4
articles.authors: Aleksei Gilev; Marina Vyrskaia; Ilia Nadporozhskii; Tommaso Valastro; Margarita Zavadskaya
articles.abstract:
This fourth wave of the Panel Study of Russian Public Opinion and Attitudes (PROPA), conducted in June 2025, provides new insights into how Russians view the economy, the war in Ukraine, political institutions, propaganda, and the country’s future.
Economic perceptions remain broadly stable. Most respondents report that their household income enables them to cover basic and some discretionary expenses, although inflation remains a persistent concern. Evaluations of both personal and national economic conditions have neither improved nor sharply deteriorated, yet perceptions of inflation and limited growth continue to pose risks to regime legitimacy.
Attitudes toward the war show a gradual but consistent erosion of open support for the “special military operation,” falling from 52% to 48% since March 2024. Meanwhile, open opposition has grown modestly, and support for peace talks has increased. War fatigue, the spillover of drone attacks into Russian cities, and the disruption of daily life appear to be contributing factors. At the same time, many respondents remain ambivalent or unwilling to state their position, reflecting ongoing fear and censorship.
Media consumption patterns highlight the effectiveness of state censorship: YouTube usage has dropped under state throttling, while reliance on Telegram for news has increased. Television remains dominant but shows signs of eroding trust. Political interest remains relatively high, though open discussion of politics has slightly declined, reflecting awareness of repression.
Approval of political institutions continues to edge downward. Support for the State Duma and government has weakened, and even Vladimir Putin’s approval shows a modest but consistent decline. Although Putin retains support from more than half of respondents, the rally-around-the-flag effect has clearly waned. Institutions such as the police and courts enjoy particularly low moral authority, while the army and the Russian Orthodox Church remain relatively trusted.
Expectations for the future are mixed. While most respondents continue to anticipate economic growth and see renewed mobilization as unlikely, optimism about a quick peace settlement has declined following unmet expectations of U.S.-mediated negotiations. Concerns about rising crime, linked to the reintegration of war veterans, are growing.
Overall, the data suggest a society that is economically stable but politically fragile. War weariness, limited trust in institutions, and declining approval of state actors all indicate that the regime’s legitimacy depends increasingly on managing perceptions of economic stability and avoiding sharp shocks that could alter public expectations.

articles.title: 《逻辑的自我反噬：论波普尔证伪论的内在悖论》 The Self-Refutation of Logic: On the Internal Paradoxes of Popper’s Falsificationism
articles.authors: songping zeng
articles.abstract:
Karl Popper’s falsificationism, which takes falsifiability as the demarcation criterion between science and non‑science, attempts to overcome the logical predicament of induction. Yet his own theory harbors irresolvable internal paradoxes. This paper systematically analyzes the multiple paradoxes of Popper’s falsificationism from three levels: its core criterion, the demarcation standard, and logical derivation. First, the core principle of falsificationism is itself not falsifiable, leading to logical self‑refutation and thus losing its legitimacy as a demarcation criterion. Second, the demarcation standard suffers from double standards, blurring the boundaries between common‑sense facts, metaphysical superstitions, and scientific propositions, thereby rendering the criterion completely ineffective. Third, it severs the connection between imaginary (logically possible) falsifiability and real (empirically possible) falsifiability, divorcing itself from the actual practice of science and degenerating into a mere logical word‑game. Popper’s falsifiability criterion deviates from the truth‑seeking essence of science, overturns the commonsense logic of induction that uses real counterexamples to refute false conclusions, classifies empirically refuted false propositions as “scientific”, and even leads to the absurd conclusion that the more counterexamples a theory has (the higher its falsifiability), the more “scientific” it becomes. It thus severs the link between a theory’s truth or falsehood and its scientific status, exposing the inherent defect of formalist logic divorced from objective empirical evidence.
The paper argues that Popper’s falsificationism is essentially a metaphysical philosophical dogma rather than a rigorous demarcation criterion for science. Its simplistic, one‑sided logical standard cannot achieve the core task of demarcating science, and ultimately collapses into self‑defeating logical inconsistency. A genuine demarcation of science must be grounded in practice, balance logic with empirical evidence, and adopt a pluralistic, comprehensive approach.

卡尔•波普尔提出的证伪主义以可证伪性作为科学与非科学的划界标准，试图突破归纳法的逻辑困境，但其理论自身潜藏着无法消解的内在悖论。本文从核心准则、划界标准、逻辑推演三个层面，系统剖析波普尔证伪论的多重悖论：其一，证伪论核心原则自身不具备可证伪性，陷入逻辑自我反噬，失去判定准则的合法性；其二，划界标准存在双重标准，混淆客观常识、玄学迷信与科学命题的边界，导致标准彻底失效；其三，割裂逻辑假想可证伪性与现实可证伪性，脱离科学实践本质，沦为纯粹逻辑文字游戏。波普尔证伪性划界标准背离科学求真的本质，颠覆了归纳法以真实反例否定谬误结论的常识逻辑，将已被事实证伪的错误命题归入科学范畴，甚至形成反例越多、可证伪性越强则越“科学”的倒置结论，既割裂了理论正误与科学属性的关联，也暴露了其形式化逻辑脱离客观实证的固有缺陷。
本研究认为，波普尔证伪论本质是形而上学哲学信条，而非严谨科学判定准则，其以偏概全的单一逻辑标准无法实现科学划界的核心目标，最终在自我否定的逻辑困局中走向失效。真正的科学划界需立足实践、兼顾逻辑与经验，实现多元综合判定。

articles.title: The Silence of Moderates Drives Social Polarization: An Agent-Based Model of Perceived and Actual Polarization
articles.authors: Kazuya Iwata; Hiroshi Shimizu
articles.abstract: Social polarization increasingly undermines the collaborative dialogue essential to democratic societies, necessitating a clearer understanding of the mechanisms behind its emergence and resolution. Traditional research has explained polarization through interpersonal repulsion, in which individuals move their opinions away from different views (e.g., Axelrod et al., 2021). While this framework focuses on conflicts among active speakers, it overlooks the silence and withdrawal of moderates. We developed a model that excludes repulsion and incorporates the silence gap effect, in which individuals remain silent toward those with stronger attitudes. Simulations show that in the early stages, the silence gap effect causes moderates to remain silent while allowing extreme opinions to dominate public expressions. This generates perceived polarization, a state in which the distribution of public opinion becomes bimodal despite unimodal internal attitudes. Subsequently, the frequently expressed extreme opinions pull other agents toward the poles, eventually leading to polarization. These findings demonstrate that the essence of polarization lies in the silence of moderates. Furthermore, our study shows that polarization can be prevented by neutralizing the silence gap effect, ensuring that moderates do not suppress opinions. However, we found that even slight differences in intervention timing determined whether future polarization was successfully averted. By contrast, interventions aimed at correcting the misperception of a polarized public failed to prevent division. Therefore, to resolve polarization, it is vital to create a dialogue environment in which moderates can speak as early as possible without apprehension, rather than directly addressing extreme voices or promoting the correction of public perception.

articles.title: Image Abstraction and Bayesian Inference: A Reconstruction of the Nature of Probability Theory from the Perspective of Cognitive Philosophy
articles.authors: Chen Lijun
articles.abstract:
This paper demonstrates, from an epistemological standpoint, the structural isomorphism between Quxiang Bilei—the classical Chinese cognitive method of image abstraction and analogical reasoning, literally "taking phenomena to form analogies and categorical associations"—and Bayesian inference. By systematically mapping the three components of Bayes's theorem—prior probability, likelihood function, and posterior probability—onto the three constitutive stages of Quxiang Bilei: image-taking, analogy-making, and verification, this paper reveals that Bayesian inference is not merely a specialized technical toolset, but rather the mathematical formalization of the metacognitive structure inherent in Quxiang Bilei.
Building upon this isomorphic framework, the paper advances five core philosophical propositions: (1) objectivity is the statistical convergence outcome of collective Bayesian updating across epistemic communities; (2) causal laws represent the limiting case in which posterior probability infinitely approaches unity; (3) the interpretability of probabilistic calculations resides not within mathematics itself, but in its correspondence with underlying metacognitive structures; (4) the absolute boundary of artificial intelligence lies not in insufficient computational power, but in its lack of embodied physical anchoring and authentic survival feedback; and (5) there exists a fundamental, non-accidental isomorphism between Quxiang Bilei and Bayesian inference.
These five propositions are not mere mutual projections between two disparate conceptual frameworks, but genuinely novel insights emerging from their productive dialogue and critical engagement. This work provides a robust epistemological foundation for Bayesianism, offers rigorous mathematical support for the modern transformation of classical Chinese epistemology, and establishes a unified analytical framework for understanding the cognitive essence and fundamental limitations of artificial intelligence.

articles.title: Toward a Framework for Digital Cultural Pedagogy: A Systematic Review of Local Wisdom and Cultural Identity Education
articles.authors: Marwan Marwan
articles.abstract: The integration of digital learning materials into local wisdom and cultural identity education has emerged as a significant focus in educational technology research, particularly in developing-country contexts where globalization threatens the transmission of indigenous and regional knowledge systems. This systematic literature review (SLR) synthesizes empirical evidence on the effectiveness, design principles, and theoretical underpinnings of digital teaching materials for local wisdom literacy and cultural identity formation among primary and secondary school students, with particular attention to Indonesian and Southeast Asian educational contexts. Following PRISMA 2020 guidelines, a systematic search was conducted across five electronic databases (Scopus, Web of Science, ERIC, Google Scholar, and DOAJ) covering the period 2014–2025. Of 1,247 initially identified records, 42 studies met the inclusion criteria following full-text screening and quality assessment using the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool (MMAT). Synthesized evidence demonstrates that digitally enhanced, culturally grounded teaching materials consistently produce significant improvements in local wisdom literacy and measurable gains in cultural identity indicators. Four design principles emerged as critical determinants of effectiveness: (1) cultural authenticity validation, (2) multimodal content integration, (3) ADDIE-based systematic development, and (4) scaffolded cultural identity engagement. Vygotsky’s socio-cultural constructivism, Mayer’s Cognitive Theory of Multimedia Learning, and Bandura’s Social Cognitive Theory constitute the most commonly applied theoretical frameworks. Rigorously developed digital teaching materials represent evidence-based, scalable instruments for culturally responsive pedagogy. Beyond synthesizing empirical evidence, this review proposes a conceptual architecture for digital cultural pedagogy integrating cultural authenticity validation, multimodal learning representation, scaffolded identity engagement, and iterative instructional evaluation. The framework provides a theoretically grounded and evidence-based foundation for culturally responsive digital education, cultural sustainability, and future curriculum innovation across diverse educational settings.

articles.title: The Preprint Problem Problem: Response to Giangrande
articles.authors: Philip N. Cohen
articles.abstract: Giangrande (2026) argues that fringe scientists have “weaponized” preprints to spread pseudoscience, and presents data to prove the point. In a response, I identify two major problems in the paper. The first is that the data do not match their description, and the second is that description of how preprint servers work – and thus the mechanism of action for the problem – is inaccurate. In addition, the documentation provided with the paper is inadequate for assessment. The paper does not establish the prevalence of weaponized pseudoscience on preprint servers beyond an existence proof. I conclude that the paper cannot live up to its inflammatory title, “The Preprint Problem” – but scientists do have an important role to play in protecting our communities from the bad actors of pseudoscience.

articles.title: Finite-Capacity Prospect Theory: State-Dependent Risk Preferences under Resource, Attention, and Boundary-Risk Constraints
articles.authors: Yining Wu
articles.abstract:
This paper develops a state-dependent version of Prospect Theory in which loss aversion, reference adaptation, and probability weighting vary with resource buffers, attention constraints, and boundary-risk context. Prospect Theory is usually treated as a descriptive account of deviations from expected-utility rationality. This paper proposes a finite-capacity bridge model: bounded agents operating under scarcity, limited attention, finite memory, and finite update capacity should exhibit state-dependent risk preferences.

The model introduces a vector-valued resource buffer, perceived distance to a constraint threshold, event-class-dependent probability weighting, and a nudge-bandwidth model. It predicts that loss aversion should rise under scarcity and downside exposure, reference baselines should adapt more slowly under depletion, and probability weighting should become more distorted for low-capacity agents and boundary-relevant rare events.

The paper includes deterministic synthetic simulations and a replication package, but does not fit human-subject data. It is intended as a theoretical working paper and empirical identification framework for future tests of state-dependent Prospect Theory.

articles.title: Chinese Automotive Finance: Development, Policies, and Regulations
articles.authors: kerry liu
articles.abstract: The rapid development of Chinese automotive, particularly in the new energy vehicle sector, have garnered global attention. This study focuses on a niche area that has yet to be thoroughly examined: automotive finance. First, it explores the evolution of automotive finance in China within the context of the broader automotive market. Second, it reviews key policy initiatives from Chinese authorities related to the automotive industry, automotive finance companies, and automotive consumption. Third, it analyzes recent regulations on automotive finance companies, concluding that these measures are designed to mitigate systemic risks within China's financial system while supporting the new energy vehicle sector.

articles.title: Beyond the Takeover: Youth Gatherings, Urban Public Space, and the Racialization of Disorder in Chicago
articles.authors: Almethia C. Franklin
articles.abstract:
In recent years, Chicago has witnessed a significant rise in large-scale youth gatherings in high-visibility public spaces. Labeled "teen takeovers" in dominant media and policy discourse, these events are almost exclusively framed as public safety threats requiring law enforcement response. This paper argues that this framing is both analytically insufficient and politically consequential. Drawing on preliminary media and news analysis, an emerging qualitative research agenda, and a multi-disciplinary theoretical framework, this paper reframes youth gatherings as social phenomena that reveal fundamental dynamics of race, space, territorial boundary, and urban belonging. We identify three dimensions that existing scholarship has not yet systematically examined: (1) the role of social media and digital technology in organizing, escalating, and documenting these events; (2) the apparent dissolution of hyperlocal territorial boundaries historically maintained by street organizations in Chicago; and (3) the variation in form, activity, and framing of analogous youth gatherings across U.S. cities. We situate these dimensions within a five-part theoretical architecture drawing on moral panic theory, critical criminology, spatial theory, Black cultural studies, and the sociology of punishment. We conclude by presenting a research agenda for empirical investigation and discussing the implications of this work for policy and community response.

Keywords: youth gatherings, teen takeovers, urban public space, moral panic, territorial boundaries, Chicago, racialization, social media, cultural criminology

articles.title: How does Coethnicity with Refugees Shape their Reception? Evidence from Afghan Refugees in Pakistan
articles.authors: Mashail Malik; Niloufer Siddiqui; Yang-Yang Zhou
articles.abstract: How does coethnicity shape host attitudes toward refugees? Existing research finds that cultural threat drives anti-refugee attitudes, so refugees who share an ethnic background with host populations should face less backlash. We examine this in Pakistan, where both refugee and host communities include substantial Pashtun populations. Drawing on survey experimental, observational, and qualitative data, we find that while a conjoint experiment shows a comparative preference for coethnics, this result masks both low absolute support and sharp internal variation. Coethnic hosts in regions where they are ethnic minorities express more inclusive attitudes than those living in their ethnic homeland. We argue that the impact of coethnicity can depend on local demography: where coethnic hosts are a marginalized minority, coethnicity with refugees can carry both instrumental and symbolic value. These findings complicate standard assumptions about the primacy of cultural threat and highlight the need to understand subnational variation in refugee-host dynamics.

articles.title: Risk Aversion with Nothing to Lose
articles.authors: Stefano Pegoraro
articles.abstract: In a continuous-time model, a risk-neutral decision-maker chooses the volatility of a state variable and is terminated when the variable falls below a threshold. I provide economically interpretable conditions under which the decision-maker becomes risk averse endogenously and minimizes volatility near termination, even if she faces myopic incentives to gamble for resurrection. The conditions introduce forward-looking incentives to preserve economic rents. I show these conditions are met in a wide range of apparently unrelated models, thus identifying forward-looking rents as a unifying economic mechanism behind endogenous risk aversion. I also provide conditions for the decision-maker to become risk loving endogenously.

articles.title: Revitalizing the County High School through University Management Delegation: Institutional Transplantation, Distributed Leadership, and Educational Outcomes in Rural China
articles.authors: Yi Duan
articles.abstract: The collapse of county-level high schools in China's less-developed regions has emerged as one of the most consequential yet under-researched dimensions of educational inequality in the twenty-first century. As urban "super schools" systematically attract the best students and most qualified teachers from surrounding counties, county schools enter a self-reinforcing cycle of quality deterioration that exacerbates rural-urban divides, undermines community cohesion, and constrains social mobility for millions of rural families. This article examines a governance innovation designed to arrest and reverse this dynamic: the university management delegation model, in which a nationally prominent university assumes formal managerial authority over a county high school through a tripartite agreement involving the national Ministry of Education, the university, and the county government. The paper provides an insider-practitioner analysis of the institutional architecture, political dynamics, and educational outcomes of this intervention. The paper theorises this reform as a case of "institutional transplantation under political facilitation," arguing that three conditions were jointly necessary for success: (1) central government authorisation providing legitimacy and protection from bureaucratic resistance; (2) political protection from within the local power structure, enabled by the dual identity of the seconded coordinator as both a national ministry official and a county standing committee member; and (3) a multi-year commitment extending to 2031, designed to institutionalise the partnership beyond individual personnel tenures. The paper identifies the specific mechanisms through which distributed leadership operated across the university team, the county government, and the school's internal management, and discusses the broader implications for education policy in resource-constrained contexts.

articles.title: Delivery Channel Design and Women's Empowerment Outcomes in Rural Vocational Training: Comparative Evidence from Two Chinese Programs
articles.authors: Yi Duan
articles.abstract: Vocational training is widely promoted as a pathway out of rural poverty in China, yet mounting evidence suggests that how programs are delivered affects outcomes as much as what they teach. This paper compares two rural hospitality training initiatives to examine how delivery channel design shapes skill transfer and empowerment. Case A examines a mountain tourism training program in Sichuan province that shifted from government administrative cascade to an academy-based, market-embedded model. Post-redesign, operators listing market prices rose from 18% to 89%, platform adoption increased from 0% to 71%, and average monthly revenue grew from approximately ¥3,200 to ¥6,100. Case B analyzes the "Digital Mulan" Homestay Butler Training Program in Jiangxi province, a public-private partnership targeting rural women through a five-day residential curriculum combining service standards, digital marketing, and cultural modules. Both cases demonstrate that shifting from administrative to experiential delivery channels improves practical competency transfer. The Digital Mulan program further shows how gender-targeted channel design contributes to empowerment beyond economic returns, including occupational identity reconstruction and social capital expansion.
