The findings carry implications for rural education policy in China and other developing contexts where training programs reach scale but struggle to generate lasting behavioral change.

articles.title: Cultural Homecoming Without Return: Elephant Symbolism, Hakka Migration, and the Ritual Regeneration of Lost Homeland
articles.authors: Yi Duan
articles.abstract: This paper examines how the Hakka people of southern Jiangxi, China, sustain a three-thousand-year cultural memory of Shang Dynasty elephant domestication through the "Nine Lions Worship Elephant" lantern festival. Drawing on archaeological evidence, paleographic analysis, historical geography, climate history, and ethnographic observation, I trace a symbol transmission chain from the Central Plains "Elephant Land" to a ritualized homecoming ceremony performed in villages that have never hosted live elephants. I argue that the elephant lantern functions as a "mnemonic vessel," a material cultural form that preserves and reactivates homeland identity by embodying an absent ecological referent, extending Connerton's inscribed and incorporated memory framework. The concept is offered as a theoretical extension, not a revolution, aimed at illuminating how displaced communities perform "ecological memory without ecological presence." The paper contributes to the anthropology of cultural memory, diaspora, and multispecies relations by showing that homeland can be sustained not as a territory but as a ritual technology.

articles.title: Institutionalizing Nature's Contributions to People: How a Chinese County Fabricated Tradeable Ecological Assets Through Sequential Governance Innovation
articles.authors: Yi Duan
articles.abstract: The gap between ecosystem services valuation and practical monetization remains a persistent governance challenge, particularly in underdeveloped regions where ecological endowments are richest and fiscal capacity is weakest. This paper presents an in depth case study of Shangyou County, Jiangxi Province, China, which sequentially constructed an ecosystem services monetization architecture through four interlocking institutional mechanisms: (1) GEP/VEP accounting that rendered ecological value legible to financial actors; (2) a centralized state owned platform that consolidated scattered ecological resources; (3) market creating instruments, including China's first collapsed hill arable land indicator trade (RMB 344,960), the nation's second soil and water conservation carbon sink trade (70,000 tons CO₂e, RMB 2.1 million), and Jiangxi Province's largest VEP backed green loan (RMB 200 million); and (4) a zero carbon industrial park that anchored long term sustainability. Drawing on the IPBES framework of Nature's Contributions to People (NCP), the analysis reveals a cumulative sequential logic in which each mechanism created the preconditions for the next: measurement enabled consolidation, consolidation enabled market creation, and market creation provided the financial and policy foundation for industrial anchoring. The case contributes to the ecosystem services literature by demonstrating that the institutionalization of NCP is not a menu of independent options but a path dependent process in which sequencing determines outcomes. The findings carry implications for Payments for Ecosystem Services design, natural capital accounting, and the governance of green finance in developing economies.

articles.title: Boundary Changes and Competitive Seat Exclusion in Singapore’s 2025 Redistricting: An Ensemble-Based Test
articles.authors: David Goh
articles.abstract:
We test whether Singapore's 2025 electoral redistricting produced a statistically anomalous allocation of competitively contested territory. Using a hypergeometric permutation test, we examine the 114 subzones that changed constituency between the 2020 and 2025 general elections.

Among the 52 subzones originating from competitive 2020 constituencies (PAP vote share < 55%), zero ended up in competitive 2025 constituencies—against an expected value of 3.2 under random redistribution (Fisher’s exact p = 0.012). The anomaly follows a specific pattern: the new competitive seat (Jalan Kayu SMC) was seeded from stronghold populations, while dissolved competitive constituencies were absorbed into safe districts.

A complementary MCMC ensemble analysis using GerryChain finds that while Singapore’s boundaries are extreme outliers in geometric compactness, this is consistent with administrative logic rather than partisan gerrymandering. We conclude that while the GRC block-vote remains the primary seat-to-vote bonus mechanism, redistricting serves as a secondary mechanism for managing competitive communities.

articles.title: Reinventing Well-being Measurements for Older Persons: Psychometric Evaluation of the Older Persons’ Well-being Scale (OPWELLS)
articles.authors: Jeanette Melin; Marit Preuter; Kristofer Årestedt; Marie-Louise Möllerberg
articles.abstract:
Purpose: To establish a set of items with good content coverage and satisfactory measurement properties for measuring older persons’ well-being; the Older Persons’ Well-being Scale (OPWELLS).
Method: A two-stage cross-sectional psychometric design was employed among older persons living in the community. In the first stage (n =441), the focus was on identifying potentially redundant items. In the second stage (n =1,882), stepwise item removal and/or alternation were conducted to optimise the balance between content coverage and measurement properties. Rasch analyses were used to evaluate measurement properties.
Results: The first-stage study revealed issues primarily related to local dependency, leading to the removal of four items prior to the second-stage study. The second-stage study yielded a set of 10 items with good content representation and satisfactory measurement properties, as well as convergent validity with general health and life satisfaction.
Conclusion: The 10-item OPWELLS provides a practical and psychometrically robust scale for measuring older persons’ well-being. Although there is no simple solution to challenges of limited content heterogeneity and insufficient measurement quality in existing well-being scales, this study represents an important step toward addressing both. By combining systematic content refinement with Rasch analysis, the OPWELLS contribute with a scale that strengthens the foundation for future research and practice.

articles.title: Executivism, the Parallel State, and the Paradoxical Path to Restoring Democratic Accountability
articles.authors: William Resh
articles.abstract: Rebuilding democratic accountability after a period of personalist consolidation requires more than reversing executive orders or reinstating dismissed personnel. This paper argues that two interlocking structural conditions, executivism and the parallel state, made personalist consolidation possible under Trumpism and will outlast any single administration. Executivism is a bipartisan institutional zeitgeist that treats the presidency as the natural locus of policy production; the parallel state is the privatized governance infrastructure through which concentrated presidential authority is exercised. Together, they reinforce one another and are bound by a campaign-finance environment that links contracting to political contribution. I sketch a reform agenda organized around three institutional sites (the civil service and contracting infrastructure, Congress, and the judiciary) and confront the paradox that these reforms must be championed by a president willing to constrain the office.

articles.title: The SIMC Framework:  Aligning Academic Research and Government Practice in Field Experiments
articles.authors: Jae Yeon Kim; Florian Keppeler
articles.abstract: Field experiments offer governments a credible route to causal evidence about what works. Yet translating experimental designs into practice remains difficult: researchers prioritize scientific rigor, while practitioners emphasize operational feasibility. We conceptualize this tension as an alignment problem. We introduce the SIMC framework (Scoping, Implementation, Measurement, and Communication), which identifies four stages where collaboration between researchers and practitioners can break down or succeed. Drawing on the COM-B model, we analyze the capability, opportunity, and motivation required to sustain collaboration. Using examples from field experiments in the U.S. and Europe, we show how trade-offs between rigor and feasibility emerge at each stage. We translate the framework into a stage-by-stage diagnostic that researchers and government agencies can apply before, during, and after fieldwork. Unlike existing guidance that treats collaboration as knowledge transfer, the SIMC framework treats it as an ongoing relational process with identifiable failure points.

articles.title: Transfiguring Smithian Liberalism: The Intellectual Legacy of Hiroshi Mizuta and Hideo Tanaka in Japan
articles.authors: JHET Assistant; Shinji Nohara
articles.abstract: Historians have noted how Smith’s name has been invoked to justify liberal economic policies in diverse national contexts. The historical elasticity and political implications of Smithian economic liberalism become clearer by examining the case of Japan. The previous studies of the reception of Smith in Japan have not sufficiently explored its liberal implications after the postwar period. Attention to these implications is essential for understanding not only the popularity of Smith studies in Japan but also the rapid growth of research on the Scottish Enlightenment from the 1970s onward, a period when the field remained comparatively small outside Britain. These two distinctive features of Japanese scholarship are closely connected, yet their relationship has not been systematically analyzed. This paper argues that this connection becomes clearer when examined through the intellectual trajectories of Hiroshi Mizuta and Hideo Tanaka, two influential scholars on the studies of Smith and the Scottish Enlightenment.

articles.title: The Line Must Be Drawn Here: Impacts of Parameter Choice on Access Disadvantage Measures
articles.authors: Willem Klumpenhouwer; Md Hamidur Rahman; Alex Karner
articles.abstract: Transportation equity researchers typically quantify either inequality (e.g., how equal are distributions between groups?) or sufficiency (e.g., how many and what kinds of people lack adequate access to the transportation resources?). Sufficiency analyses offer more actionable insights that can be used to mitigate disadvantage, but fundamental analytical methods for sufficiency analyses are not well developed. To advance this area of research and practice, this paper investigates three approaches to measuring sufficiency through the lens of public transport access to jobs: (i) fraction of total regional destinations reachable, (ii) competitiveness with auto access, and (iii) population-weighted percentile measures. We use a class of decomposable Foster-Greer-Thorbecke poverty measures to understand the sensitivity of overall levels of disadvantage to the choice of disadvantage lines (sufficiency thresholds) and other parameters, in the context of seven U.S. urban regions. We find that fractional and auto competitiveness measures produce similar results and are highly sensitive to the choice of disadvantage line, population-weighted percentile measures may allow for better comparisons across demographic groups, and by most reasonable definitions the vast majority of residents (80+%) in an area might be considered to experience access disadvantage.

articles.title: Thomas Edward Cliffe Leslie and the political economy of British land systems: From the critique of the classical landed property reform to the birth of Irish historical economics
articles.authors: JHET Assistant; Manolis Manioudis
articles.abstract: Thomas Edward Cliffe Leslie is an influential figure in the Irish history of economic thought. This paper presents Leslie’s critical appraisal of British land systems and discusses his critique towards the classical landed property reform. The paper illustrates the affinities between Leslie’s and John Stuart Mill's economic thought regarding the Irish land question. Furthermore, it shows that Leslie’s support for small proprietorship in Ireland is regarded as a prerequisite for defending the Act of Union of 1800. Lastly, the paper argues that Leslie’s political economy of British land systems is a significant stream in the foundation of the Irish historical school and in the promotion of an alternative political economy.

articles.title: TRAJETÓRIAS ESCOLARES NO BRASIL ENTRE 2008 E 2018: UMA ANÁLISE DE SEQUÊNCIA
articles.authors: Estevão de Almeida Vilela; Bernardo L Queiroz
articles.abstract: Este artigo analisa as trajetórias escolares da coorte de estudantes ingressantes no 2º ano do ensino fundamental no Brasil em 2008, a partir de uma perspectiva de Curso de Vida Educacional, utilizando do arcabouço de análise de sequências. A classificação das trajetórias escolares tem sido realizada de forma arbitrária e menos eficaz em criar tipificações que caracterizam a complexidade do evento. Os resultados do artigo identificam padrões de trajetórias que revelam estudantes com percursos escolares estáveis e outros marcados por repetências e evasão em diferentes etapas da educação básica. Os resultados encontrados evidenciam a complexidade das trajetórias no Brasil e os padrões contribuem para um diagnóstico mais preciso da realidade educacional.

articles.title: A Measurement Framework for Detecting High-Velocity Events in Social Media
articles.authors: Peter C. Baker
articles.abstract: This paper presents a measurement framework for detecting high-velocity events in social media using time-resolved post tracking. Rather than relying on static snapshots, the framework repeatedly samples posts over time and reconstructs their growth trajectories from timestamped observations. From these trajectories, rate-based metrics are derived to capture changes in engagement, including score and comment velocity. High-velocity events are defined as posts exhibiting extreme short-term growth rates relative to a fixed threshold, enabling systematic identification of burst-like behavior. Application of the framework to Reddit data shows that a small subset of posts exhibits rapid, high-intensity growth, while the majority evolve more gradually. These findings demonstrate that high-velocity events can be detected using simple, interpretable metrics, providing a foundation for studying temporal concentration and extreme dynamics in online systems.

articles.title: Inflectional Paradigms as a Diagnostic Tool for Tokenizers in Morphologically Rich Languages: A Proposal for a Linguistic Benchmark
articles.authors: Elżbieta Dawidek
articles.abstract:
This article addresses the problem of evaluating the morphological quality of tokenizers used in natural language processing models for synthetic languages. The starting point is the observation that the standard measure of fertility ratio — the mean number of tokens per inflected form — captures the problem of fragmentation quantitatively but does not diagnose its morphological structure. A tokenizer achieving a low fertility ratio may nonetheless systematically cut morpheme boundaries at grammatically significant points, destroying information about the lexical stem, inflectional suffixes, or derivational prefixes.
Based on empirical analysis of four tokenizers with different architectures (BPE, WordPiece, Unigram LM) and training corpus compositions (monolingual Polish, monolingual English, multilingual), conducted on 109 Polish inflected forms across full paradigms of 10 lexemes, this article proposes a linguistic benchmark based on three metrics: stem integrity (SI), inflectional suffix separation (ISS), and mean fragmentation per lexeme (MFL). Results show a 3.2x ratio between the monolingual Polish tokenizer (HerBERT BPE-PL, MFL = 1.22) and the monolingual English tokenizer (GPT-2 BPE-EN, MFL = 3.91), and indicate that tokenizer architecture has independent explanatory power: XLM-R Unigram (MFL = 1.66) with a multilingual training corpus approaches the monolingual tokenizer more closely than mBERT WordPiece (MFL = 2.45) with comparable data composition.
The proposed benchmark is designed as minimal yet sufficient: aspect and conditional mood do not require separate diagnostic categories, because their behavior follows structurally from the behavior of the prefix and suffix that the benchmark measures directly. Inferring these categories from a correctly tokenized form is a task for the language model, not the tokenizer. This article continues research published in the preprint Dawidek (2026a) and constitutes a proof of concept for an evaluation tool that fills the gap between performance-based and linguistic assessment of tokenizers in morphologically rich languages.

articles.title: Strategic Heterogeneity in Issue Positioning: Evidence from Congressional Campaigns
articles.authors: Colin Rafferty Case; Rachel Porter
articles.abstract: Do candidates maintain consistent left-right issue positions in congressional elections? How do district conditions influence variations in this consistency? While these questions are fundamental to the study of U.S. elections, surprisingly little is known about issue positioning in campaigns. We combine campaign platforms with machine learning methods to estimate the left-right orientations of U.S. House candidates (n = 4,509) across six salient issue areas in multiple election cycles (2018-2022). Our validated measures reveal meaningful within-candidate variation in issue positioning that systematically reflects district conditions. Candidates exhibit greater positional heterogeneity in districts that are not safe for their party and where constituent policy preferences are variable. We further demonstrate that this positional variation directly reflects district opinion, as issue-specific positioning aligns with constituency policy preferences. These findings reveal how candidate messaging varies systematically across issue domains and electoral contexts, fundamentally shaping the information environment that structures democratic choice and representation.

articles.title: 100 ANOS DA REVOLUÇÃO RUSSA: ENSINAMENTOS DA ATUAÇÃO DOS ANARQUISTAS
articles.authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes; Fernando Antonio da Costa Vieira
articles.abstract: O presente artigo procura apresentar, nos 100 anos de comemoração da Revolução Russa, uma análise histórica da participação anarquista nos processos pré-revolução e durante os anos de Comunismo de Guerra. Em um segundo momento, a pesquisa busca descrever o principal movimento anarquista durante a Revolução Russa, ou seja, a atuação de Nestor Ivánovitch Makhno e seu Exército Negro na Ucrânia. Por fim, o estudo almeja fazer uma correlação dos fatos históricos vividos naquele período, pelos grupos de esquerda, com a atual conjuntura brasileira.

articles.title: Perceptions of the Ideal Mother: An Experimental Investigation
articles.authors: Nevena Kulic; Maria-Valentina Stoicescu; Anne-Marie Therese Jeannet; Giulia Dotti Sani
articles.abstract: The normative framework that defines ideal motherhood, prescribing what it means to be a “good mother” in a given cultural and historical context, significantly influences women’s career aspirations, family choices, and fertility decisions. Yet, there is limited understanding of what women themselves consider to be “ideal motherhood”, particularly given that such ideals may reflect both genuine preferences and adaptations to structural constraints. Gaining insight into both personal and perceived societal ideals is crucial for evaluating the extent to which women’s views of motherhood align with, or diverge from, prevailing social expectations. This study builds on hegemonic and non-hegemonic models of motherhood differing in the degree of maternal involvement, the role of maternal employment, and the division of childcare responsibilities. We conduct a large-scale conjoint experiment embedded in an online survey administered in autumn 2024, involving approximately 4,500 Italian women aged 20 to 50, to evaluate the relative importance of various attributes associated with different models of motherhood. The findings show that women tend to favor elements of counter-hegemonic motherhood models over hegemonic ones, both in their personal ideals and in their perceptions of societal expectations. In particular, maternal employment (whether full- or part-time) and the sharing of childcare responsibilities with fathers emerge as central components of the ideal mother. These results challenge conventional assumptions about women’s endorsement of the dominant notion of mothering, offering new insight into shifting motherhood ideals in contemporary Italy.

articles.title: Concentrated Affluence: The Spatial Segregation of High-Income Households across Richmond, VA
articles.authors: Billy Southern
articles.abstract:
Researchers examining issues of inequality focus overwhelmingly on racially segregated neighborhoods, concentrated poverty, and spatial isolation. Such focus is appropriate, given that spatial concentration amplifies the harms of poverty. But this overemphasis has driven policymakers to prioritize mitigating concentration, as evidenced by numerous dispersal policies. In contrast, scholars pay far less attention to the opposite extreme: the concentration of affluent households. The spatial concentration of affluence enables advantaged households to hoard opportunity, skew resource distribution, limit access to public amenities, and consolidate political power. Across public discourse, concentrated affluence is routinely treated as neutral, and policymakers rarely pursue strategies to confront this consequential component of segregation. 

This study focuses on the segregation of affluence, examining changes in the concentration of high-income households since 1990. Using Richmond, Virginia, as a case study, findings demonstrate that affluent households are the most unevenly distributed group, rapidly segregating in the city and growing more concentrated in the suburbs. This paper makes two contributions: First, it captures changes in the geography of poverty and affluence by tracking low- and high-income households. Second, it provides an intraurban analysis of segregation, detailing household sorting across urban and suburban spaces. Growing spatial segregation of affluent households reveals a planning system that privileges the advantaged, and a focus on concentrated affluence is essential for urban policy to address spatial inequality.

articles.title: Policing the Screen, Screening the Policed: Immigrant Representation, Surveillance, and Racial Capitalism in Icelandic Cinema
articles.authors: Armando Garcia
articles.abstract:
Abstract:
This article develops an anthropological reading of immigrant representation in Icelandic cinema through the lens of racial capitalism. Focusing on Ísold Uggadóttir's Andið eðlilega / And Breathe Normally (2018), alongside Tryggð / The Deposit (2019), Gullregn (2020), Kona fer í stríð / Woman at War (2018), Ikíngut (2000), Ófærð / Trapped (2015–), and a wider corpus of films and series, it argues that recent Icelandic audio-visual production renders visible the surveillance and policing infrastructures through which racialised mobility is governed at the Nordic periphery. The film analysis is brought into dialogue with empirical research on police–minority relations in Iceland, which documents perceptions of bias and procedural injustice among persons of colour and migrants, and with scholarship on whiteness, Nordic exceptionalism, and crimmigration in the wider region. Drawing on a small but theoretically informed film corpus and a typology of immigrant subjectivities, the article shows how cinematic framings of asylum seekers, Indigenous children, low-budget tourists and migrant workers both reproduce and contest Icelandic narratives of innocence. The article concludes with policy implications for anti-racist reform of Icelandic border and policing practices, and for public support of migrant-centred film-making.

articles.title: Negative shocks travel home: the income elasticity of temporary migrant remittances
articles.authors: Ryan Barclay Edwards; Estelle Stambolie
articles.abstract: Using high-frequency financial diaries from Fijian temporary migrant workers in Australia and their families back home, we estimate the within-individual income elasticity of remittances at approximately 0.3 and a marginal propensity to consume remittance income at around 0.7. Migrants send regular, stable amounts home, regardless of their earnings level: remittance budget shares fall with income in our sample, resembling Engel's Law. Exploiting fortnightly variation within individuals, we find that the contemporaneous elasticity is driven by negative shocks, while positive shocks are absorbed with higher consumption or savings abroad. The immediate pass-through of earnings declines to families, for whom remittances are typically the primary and often only source of income, has direct implications for worker welfare protections and the vulnerability of remittance-receiving households.

articles.title: From Fire to Algorithms: Why Fear of AI Is Not New, But Still Matters
articles.authors: Nazia Riasat
articles.abstract:
Eight in ten researchers already use AI tools in their work. Most will not say so out loud in a
conference room. This paper examines that gap, the space between what academics do privately
and what they defend publicly and argues that it is costing the research community something real.
Using documented adoption data across medicine, education, coding, and creative industries,
alongside historical cases from the introduction of writing to the Industrial Revolution, the paper
makes the case that the fear surrounding AI in academia is not new, but the silence around it is a
choice. And it is a choice with consequences.
Keywords: Artificial Intelligence, Academic Culture, Research Methodology, AI Adoption, Higher
Education, Technology Adoption.
